"That's why everything falls apart for you. You always have to leave some cushion—for human error."
Question:
Why didn't the builder take into account that the assistant's mother might get sick?
MAIN:
There are no perfect people. There are plans that take into account that people can make mistakes.
________________


Chapter 2.37 A Tale of Two Diggers
(Why don't most people succeed?)
Two diggers were digging holes. One was diligent, the other was lazy.
The diligent one would get up at 5 a.m. and dig until evening, with calluses on his hands and sweat pouring down. The lazy one would get up at 9 a.m., dig for an hour, rest for two, dig for an hour, rest for two.
A month later, we looked at the results. The diligent one dug 10 holes. The lazy one dug 3 holes.
Everyone thought that the diligent one was a good guy, and the lazy one was a backward one.
But a year later, it turned out that the lazy man had stopped digging holes. He invented the excavator. Now he sits in the cab and digs 100 holes in an hour. And the diligent man still digs with a shovel.
Question:
Who was actually smarter?
MAIN:
Don't confuse sweat on your forehead with forward movement.
Sometimes it's better to sit down and think than to get up and run.
________________


Chapter 2.38 The Chicken and Egg Story (Diversification)
There was a man who had one hen. A good hen, she laid an egg every day.
The man was happy, sold eggs, and lived happily.
But one day the hen got sick and stopped laying eggs. The man was left penniless.
He went to his neighbor to ask for advice. The neighbor had three hens. One was laying eggs, the second was brooding, and the third was just wandering around.
"Why do you have three?" the man asked. "Are you missing some balls?"
"Enough," the neighbor replied. "But if one gets sick, I have two more. I won't be left without eggs."
The man thought about it. He returned home and bought two more chickens. And never again was he left without eggs.
Question:
Why did the neighbor turn out to be smarter?
MAIN:
One chicken is risk. Three chickens are stability.
Don't put all your eggs in one basket. 
________________


Chapter 2.39 The Black Swan That Changed Everything
(Black swans)
White swans lived in a pond. They swam, dived, caught fish, and thought it would always be like this. The sun was shining, the water was warm, and there was plenty of food. They couldn't even imagine that things could be any other way.
"There are only white swans in the world," they said. "It's the law of nature."
But one day, a black swan swam onto the pond. He was huge, beautiful, and completely different from all the others.
The white swans were frightened:
— Who are you? Where did you come from? We've never seen anyone like you!
The Black Swan smiled:
"I lived somewhere else, far away from here. We're all like that there."
And suddenly everything changed. The water grew colder, the fish disappeared, the wind blew stronger. The white swans didn't know what to do. They were used to everything being fine and weren't prepared for difficulties.
And the black swan said:
"I didn't come to scare you. I came so you know: the world isn't always as you're used to seeing it. Sometimes something new, unexpected, comes along, and everything changes. You have to be prepared for that."
Question:
Are you prepared for the fact that one day a “black swan” might appear in your life – something unexpected, new and not always positive?
MAIN:
There are more than just white swans in the world. Sometimes black ones come too. Be prepared.
________________


Chapter 2.40: A Tale of Three Athletes (Antifragility)
Three athletes trained in one section.
The first one was fragile. When the coach gave him a difficult exercise, he would lose heart, whine, and complain of pain. And after his first injury, he quit training altogether. His career was over before it even began.
The second one was strong. He endured, didn't whine, and did everything he was told. But he didn't get stronger—he simply remained the same. Neither better nor worse. Strong, but not growing.
The third was flexible. He fell, too. He got injured, too. But after each fall, he got up, analyzed what he had done wrong, and trained even harder. Every mistake made him stronger. Every failure, wiser. Every injury, more careful, but not weaker.
Ten years later, the first was working as a salesman in a sporting goods store, telling customers how hard it was to be an athlete. The second became a coach in the same sporting goods store—not bad, but average. And the third won gold at the Olympics.
“How did you manage to do that?” they asked him.
"I wasn't afraid of falling," he replied. "I was afraid of not getting up. Every blow made me stronger because I learned from it."
Question:
Which of the three athletes do you think was successful and why?
Main:
It doesn't matter how many times you fall.
What matters is how many times you got up and became better than you were before you fell.
________________
Chapter 2.41 The story of Uncle Borya, who got rich
(Big money = big problems)
Uncle Borya lived in a certain town. He was a simple mechanic, fixing faucets and pipes. He had enough money for food, an apartment, a small car, and fishing on weekends. And Uncle Borya was happy.
One day he bought a lottery ticket and won a million rubles!
At first, Uncle Borya jumped for joy. Then he ran to his wife:
— Get ready, let's go to Turkey! I'll buy you a fur coat! A new car!
And a week later the problems started.
First came some "old friends" Uncle Borya hadn't seen in ages. One asked for a loan for an operation, another for a business venture, and a third just for fun, "until payday." Uncle Borya wasn't one to refuse—he handed over half a million.
Then the bandits came. They said, "We have to share." Uncle Borya got scared and gave another two hundred thousand.
Then the tax office came: "Where's the declaration? Where are the taxes?" I had to pay a fine.
A month later, distant relatives from another city showed up. An auntie Uncle Borya had never even seen demanded, "You're rich now, help me!"
By the end of the month, a million was down to a hundred thousand. Uncle Borya was gloomy. His wife was nagging him. The friends who had received the money had disappeared. The bandits promised to come again.
Uncle Borya sat in the kitchen and sighed:
— I wish I hadn't bought that ticket. We were living peacefully...
Question:
What do you think Uncle Borya did wrong?
What should have been done with the money?
MAIN:
Big money does not mean freedom, but new challenges and risks.
They destroy without preparation.
________________


Chapter 2.41.1 The Tale of Two Kings and Their Treasures
(The Public Billionaire Trap)
In one kingdom there lived two kings. Both received enormous treasures.
The first king, named Severus, hid all his gold in a deep cave behind seven locks. He dressed simply, lived in a small house, and told no one about his riches. When someone asked why he lived so modestly, Severus replied, "Why should I show off my gold? I already know I have it. And as long as no one knows about it, no one will come to take it away."
The second king, named South, took a different approach. He built a vast palace with golden domes. Every day, he rode out to the square in a carriage adorned with precious stones. The people admired him; guests came from all over the world to see South's wealth. But along with this admiration came other guests. Tax collectors came and said, "Since you're so rich, pay ten times as much into the treasury." Bandits came and began plotting how to break into the palace. Envious neighbors came and began plotting intrigues.
One day, a messenger came to the North and told him that the King of the South had been captured and imprisoned. The North sighed and said, "He chose to show off. I chose to rule. These are two different paths."
Question:
Why do you think King North hid his wealth, while King South put it on display?
Which of them ended up safer?
MAIN:
You can own a treasure. Or you can show it off.
But you can almost never do both at the same time.
________________


Chapter 2.42: The Tale of the Echo in the Mountains (The "Eye for an Eye" Strategy)
A boy went with his grandfather to the mountains. They walked for a long time, and the boy got tired. He got bored and decided to make some noise.
He took a deep breath and shouted into the void:
- Hey-hey!
And the mountains answered him in kind: "Hey-hey!"
The boy liked it. He shouted:
- You are good!
And the mountains answered: "You are good!"
Then the boy decided to test what would happen if he shouted something angry. He frowned and shouted:
- You're a fool!
And the mountains answered: "You are a fool!"
The boy was offended. He tugged his grandfather's sleeve:
- Grandpa, someone is calling you names!
Grandfather smiled and said:
"It's not someone. It's your own voice. Mountains always return exactly what you send them. Shout out something good, and you receive something good. Shout out something bad, and don't be offended; you sent it there yourself."
The boy thought. Then he took another breath and shouted:
- I'm sorry, I was wrong!
And the mountains answered: “Forgive me, I was wrong!”
From then on, the boy always remembered: mountains don't get angry, mountains don't take revenge. They simply return what you gave them.
Question:
Why do you think the mountains responded to the boy with the same things he shouted at them?
MAIN:
Whatever you put out into the world, it will return to you. Don't be angry with the echo.
________________


Chapter 2.43 A Tale of Two Aquariums
(Two types of people and transparency of relationships)
There were two aquariums in one house.
In the first aquarium, the water was murky and dirty. The fish swam erratically, colliding with each other, and not knowing where they belonged. They often fought.
In the second aquarium, the water was crystal clear. All the fish were visible, each knew its place, swam in orderly rows, and no one disturbed anyone else.
The aquarium owner explained:
"In murky water, no one can see anyone else; everyone is afraid of everything, that's why they quarrel. But in clear water, everything is visible, everyone is calm."
Question:
What kind of aquarium would you like to live in?
MAIN:
Transparency in relationships and management removes fear and chaos.
Muddy waters are conflicts.
________________


Chapter 2.44 The Story of the Pumpkin Field (The Pumpkin Method)
A farmer had a field with pumpkins growing on it.
The farmer watered every pumpkin in a row, fertilized every one, and tended them all equally. The pumpkins grew, but they were all small and unsightly. No one bought them.
Another farmer also had a field. But he did it differently.
He chose the most promising pumpkin and devoted all his energy to it. He watered it exclusively, fertilized it exclusively, and protected it from weeds. He barely touched the other pumpkins.
By autumn, he had grown a giant pumpkin. So huge that all the neighbors came to see it. It was purchased for a large sum of money for an exhibition.
“And the other pumpkins?” they asked him.
"And the rest are for cattle feed," the farmer replied. "But one of them made me famous throughout the entire region."
Question:
Which farmer turned out to be smarter?
MAIN:
You can't grow a giant pumpkin if you water the entire field.
________________


Chapter 2.45 The Tale of Three Masters and One Cart
(The Power of Teamwork)
Three craftsmen worked in one village workshop.
The first one made wheels. He selected the strongest wood, turned the spokes, and covered the rims with iron. His wheels lasted for years and didn't fall apart over bumps.
The second one made axles. His work was unnoticeable, but without it, the wheels couldn't turn. He selected the perfect size so there was no play, but also no jamming. His axles didn't creak or break.
The third was an assembler. He took the finished wheels and axles, connected them to the body, and checked that the cart ran smoothly, didn't lean, and didn't fall apart at the first turn.
One day they argued about which of them was the most important.
"Without my wheels the cart won't move!" said the first one.
"Without my axles, the wheels will simply fall off as soon as you load them!" the second one objected.
"And without me, you're both just a pile of wood," the third one chuckled. "I'm bringing your work together into one whole."
They were offended and went their separate ways. Each decided to make a cart themselves.
The first one turned out some beautiful wheels. But when he tried to attach them to the body, he realized he didn't know how to make an axle. The cart stood on its wheels, but it wouldn't move.
The second one made perfect axles. But without wheels, they were just iron bars. There was nothing to attach to them.
The third assembled the body, but without wheels and axles it remained just a box on the ground.
A month later they met, tired and having achieved nothing.
"We behaved like children," said the first. "Everyone was pulling for themselves."
"Let's work together," suggested the second one. "You make the wheels, I'll make the axles, you assemble them."
They got back to work. The carts started moving. The money started flowing.
Question:
Why do you think the craftsmen couldn't make the cart alone, but together they could?
Main:
On a team, everyone is important. Even the strongest one is a true warrior alone. Together, they are masters.
________________


Chapter 2.46 The Balloon Story (Five Faiths)
A hot air balloon was flying. Beautiful, big, bright. The pilot sat in the basket, enjoying life.
Suddenly the wind picked up and the ball began to be thrown from side to side.
The pilot was thinking only about this:
"I believe the ball will hold up! It's strong!" That was self-confidence.
"I believe in the company that made it! They're professionals!"—faith in the manufacturer.
"I believe in the laws of physics! The ball can't fall if the burner is working!" — faith in the system.
"I believe in my passengers! They're not panicking, they're not twitching!"—faith in the crew.
"I believe this flight is necessary! That I'm not flying in vain!"—faith in the mission.
And the pilot calmed down. And the balloon held up. And they all, along with the passengers, reached their destination.
But what if even one faith had failed? What if the pilot had doubted himself? Or the balloon? Or why he was flying at all? His hands would have dropped, and the balloon would have crashed.
Question:
How much faith does it take to move calmly and steadily in any project?
MAIN:
All Five Faiths are needed for stable forward movement.
Without any of them, nothing will work.
________________


Chapter 2.47 The Story of Two Merchant Brothers
(Two types of promotion)
Two brothers were trading at the market. They sold the same goods, in the same row. But they sold them differently.
The older brother, Ivan, would come out to the counter every morning and start shouting:
— Come on in, grab some! The best merchandise! The lowest prices!
He'd grab passersby by the sleeves, drag them in, and practically force them to buy. By evening, he'd be exhausted, his voice hoarse, but he still sold a lot.
The younger brother, Pyotr, sat quietly. He simply arranged his wares beautifully, smiling at passersby, but never grabbed anyone. Sometimes people approached him with questions, and he calmly answered, telling stories, demonstrating.
At the end of the month, we compared revenue. Ivan's was 100,000. Petr's was 80,000.
"You see," said Ivan. "I sold more."
"Wait," Peter replied. "How much did you spend on screaming? Your voice trembled, you bought some candy. You ate more because of the stress. You tore your shirt. Your net profit is 90,000. And mine is 80,000, and I was sitting quietly, drinking tea."
"So what?" Ivan persisted. "I still earned more."
"Just wait," Peter smiled. "Those you grabbed by the sleeves won't come back to you. But they come to me again and again. And they bring friends. In a year, I'll have 200 thousand, and you'll have the same 90."
Question:
Which brother will be the winner in a year?
MAIN:
A scream sells once. A smile sells a lifetime.
________________


Chapter 2.48 The Story of Vovochka and Three Letters
(The "3-message" method)
Vovochka fell in love with a girl from a parallel class. She was beautiful, smart, and an excellent student. But Vovochka was a poor student and a troublemaker.
Friends advised:
— Go up to her and say directly: "Let's be friends." What's the point of dragging it out?
Vovochka did just that. He came up to me during recess and said:
- Masha, let's be friends!
Masha twirled her finger at her temple and left.
Vovochka was upset. He went to his older brother for advice. His brother was smart and went to college.
"Are you stupid or something?" said the brother. "Who does that? It should be done differently."
And he taught Vovochka the three-letter method.
Letter One:
Vovochka wrote Masha a note: "Masha, you answered so well on the test today. I didn't even understand how you did it. Respect."
Masha was surprised, but answered: “Thank you.”
Letter two (three days later):
"Masha, I'm trying to solve a math problem here, and I'm having trouble. Can you help me? You're smart."
Masha helped. Vovochka thanked her.
Letter three (a couple of days later):
"Masha, thank you so much for your help. I couldn't have done it without you. Listen, maybe we should go to the movies on Saturday? I'm treating you."
Masha agreed.
Question:
Why did Masha agree after the third letter, but refuse after the first?
MAIN:
Make friends first, then propose.
________________


Chapter 2.49 The Story of the Silent Salesman
(Six Priorities + Five Golden Questions)
A man walked into the store. He looked at the display case, sighed, and got ready to leave.
The salesman, an experienced guy, came up to me and instead of the usual “what do you want?” asked:
— Tell me, what is most important to you right now?
The man was surprised, but answered:
— I don’t know... Probably so that the children don’t need anything.
"Why is this so important to you?" the salesman asked.
— Because I didn't have a lot of things as a child. I don't want mine to have the same.
"I understand," the salesman nodded. "But what if you can't give it to them?"
The man's face darkened:
— I'll feel like a worthless father. Like I failed.
- And how long has this been bothering you?
— All my life, probably.
The seller was silent. Then he said:
"I have something that will help you earn more. Not today, but in six months. If you're willing to work, of course. Should I tell you?"
The man nodded. And after six months, he really did start earning more.
Question:
Why did the man agree to buy even though the seller wasn't selling anything?
MAIN:
Don't sell. Help the person want to buy.
________________


Chapter 2.50 The Story of the Persistent Salesman
(Six touches)
A man came into a store to look at televisions. Just to look. Maybe he'll buy one, maybe not.
The seller jumped up to him:
— What do you want? This one is good, this one is even better, this one is the best. Buy it!
The man politely refused and left.
The seller was upset:
— It didn't work.
A week later the man came again. He looked, walked around, and left again.
The seller didn’t come back anymore – he was offended.
A month later, the man came back for the third time. He walked around, looked, and bought it.
The seller was surprised:
— Why did you buy it today and not last time?
"Because last time I was just looking around. I had to get used to the idea that I even wanted a TV. Then choose a model. Then compare prices. Then consult with my wife. This doesn't happen overnight."
Question:
How many times do you have to show a product to someone before they buy it?
MAIN:
For a person to buy, at least six touches with the product are needed.
Don't give up after the first rejection.
________________


Chapter 2.51 The Story of a Student and a Professor
(Six degrees of separation)
A student dreamed of getting into a graduate program with a renowned professor. But the professor was in another city, at another university, and the student had no idea how to approach him.
He went to his supervisor:
"Ivan Petrovich, help me. I want to see Professor Sokolov, but I don't know how to contact him."
"Oh, I studied under Sokolov!" said Ivan Petrovich. "I can give you the phone number of his former graduate student; he's in Moscow now."
The student called his former graduate student. He said:
"Sokolov rarely answers his calls these days. But I know his wife; we were at a conference together. I can give her email."
The student wrote to the professor's wife. She replied:
- My husband is very busy now, but if you want, I will give him your letter.
A week later, Professor Sokolov himself called the student:
— Hello, my wife gave me your letter. Tell me about the work.
The student told the story. The professor liked it. A year later, the student was already in graduate school.
Question:
How many people helped the student get to the professor?
MAIN:
Any person on the planet is no more than six handshakes away.
Just start looking.
________________


Chapter 2.52: The Story of the Road with a Blockage
(Quick adaptation to changes)
People were walking along the road. Suddenly, there was a blockage ahead. Rocks, trees, no way through.
Some people stopped and cried:
— Everything is lost! There is no road! We can’t go any further!
Other people sat down and waited for the rubble to be cleared. They waited a day, two, a week—no one did.
The third group looked around, found a detour, and followed it. It was a difficult path, but they continued on.
A month later, they reached their destination. Those who had cried and waited remained at the rubble.
Question:
Which people turned out to be the smartest?
MAIN:
A crisis is not a time to retreat. It's a time to find a way around it. 
________________


Chapter 2.53: The Story of Ashot and His Shawarma
(Personal brand and target audience)
Ten shawarma places opened in one city. All the same: lavash, chicken, vegetables, sauce. Prices were roughly the same. And they all stood around waiting for customers.
But there was one spot where there was always a line. Ashot was standing there.
Ashot didn't just wrap shawarma. He smiled at every customer. He remembered each customer's favorite sauce. He asked about their health, their children, their work. He even let them try new sauces for free.
People weren't going for shawarma. People were going to see Ashot.
When the 11th location opened, the owners were surprised:
"Why is there a line at Ashot's and ours is empty? We make the same shawarma!"
They sent a secret shopper. He took the shawarma from Ashot and brought it to them. They tried it—it was exactly the same. Even the sauce was the same.
Then they approached Ashot and asked:
"Listen, what's the secret? You have the same product as everyone else."
Ashot smiled:
"You have the product. I have me. People don't come to eat shawarma. People come to be noticed."
Question:
What did people buy from Ashot – shawarma or attention?
MAIN:
People choose people, not services. You can be copied, but your attitude cannot.
Everyone's shawarma is the same. Attentive and good-natured Ashot is the only one.
Become Ashot.
________________


Chapter 2.54 The Story of the Three-Legged Horse
(Competencies + Resources + Money)
The blacksmith had a horse. Beautiful, strong, fast. But it limped on one leg.
The blacksmith shod her three healthy legs. The horse ran faster. But the fourth leg still hurt, and the horse couldn't gallop for long.
A sage came and said:
"You're treating the healthy legs. But the bad one remains bad. The horse won't gallop until you heal all four."
The blacksmith cured the sore leg. The horse galloped.
"Why didn't I realize this before?" the blacksmith asked in surprise. "All legs are important."
Question:
Why couldn't the horse gallop fast even when three legs were healthy?
MAIN:
A horse gallops on four legs. If one hurts, you won't get far.
________________


Chapter 2.55: The Story of a Photographer and a Neural Network
(Tools: Content, Neural Networks and Automation)
Once upon a time, there was a photographer. He shot weddings, corporate events, and children's parties. He worked a lot, but somehow, there was no passion. He had enough money, but there wasn't enough left for much else.
One day his nephew, a student programmer, came to him and said:
— Dude, why do you only have 500 subscribers online? You're such a great videographer!
"And when do I have time to do this?" the photographer asked, surprised. "I spend all day shooting weddings, then I edit them, and then I sleep."
The nephew sighed, took out his laptop and set up neural networks for him in an hour.
"Look," he said. "Now you don't have to write your own posts. The neural network writes them for you. No need to come up with ideas—the neural network generates 100 ideas in a minute. No need to spend hours processing each photo—the neural network does it in seconds."
The photographer didn't believe it, but decided to try.
Within six months, he had 50,000 subscribers. People were lining up to see him six months in advance. He tripled his prices, but his clientele remained the same.
"What's the secret?" my colleagues asked. "You used to shoot so-so stuff."
"I still shoot the same way," the photographer smiled. "It's just that no one knew about me before. But now they do. Because I talk about my work every day. Not with my hands, but with neural networks. But I do talk about it."
Question:
What has changed in the photographer's life—his ability to take pictures or his ability to talk about himself?
MAIN:
You don't have to do it yourself. Find a way to have it done for you. 
________________


Chapter 2.56: The Story of a Boss Who Did Everything Himself
(Delegation)
There was a boss. A good, responsible one. But he had one flaw: he didn't trust anyone.
I wrote the reports myself, answered the letters myself, called the clients myself, checked the quality myself, and did the accounting myself.
I worked from 8 a.m. until midnight. I never took a day off. My wife complained, my children were upset, and my health was deteriorating.
One day, he collapsed from exhaustion. While he was in the hospital, his deputy, whom he had never trusted, took over.
When the boss returned a month later, he expected to find ruins. But everything worked. Even better than when he'd been there.
“How did you do it?” he asked.
"I just did what you taught me," the deputy replied. "I only got more done because I didn't sweat the small stuff."
And then the boss realized that all these years he'd simply been afraid to let go of control. But as soon as he did, the system began to breathe.
Question:
Why did the boss work more and achieve less than his deputy?
MAIN:
If you do everything yourself, you're simply the busiest person in the cemetery. Delegate. 
________________


Chapter 2.57 A Tale of Two Builders
(Speed ​​is more important than quality at the start)
Two builders were building a house. One was a perfectionist, the other a pragmatist.
The perfectionist checked every angle, adjusted every plank, sanded every surface. The house was turning out perfect, but he'd been building it for three years.
The pragmatist worked quickly. Straight where necessary, a little crooked where possible, but quickly. A year later, the house was ready, and he moved in.
The perfectionist looked at his house and grimaced:
— The angles aren't 90 degrees, the floor creaks, the roof is slightly uneven. Is it possible to live like this?
"Yes, yes," the pragmatist replied. "I'm already living there. And by the time you finish your perfect house, I'll have lived in this one for five years. And in that time, I'll have earned enough money for another house."
Question:
Which builder is smarter?
MAIN:
Better (at the start) quickly and crookedly than perfectly, but never. 
________________


Chapter 2.58 The Story of the Bridge Builder (Feedback)
In a certain town there lived a builder. He built bridges. Good, reliable, beautiful ones. Everyone praised him.
One day a customer came to him and said:
— Build me a bridge across the river. Here are the plans, here is the money.
The builder built it. The bridge turned out great. But for some reason, the client was dissatisfied.
"What's wrong?" the builder asked in surprise. "I did everything according to the drawings!"
"Did you ask me what I really wanted?" the customer replied. "I didn't need a bridge. I needed a cattle crossing. And you built a car crossing. The cattle are afraid to cross it."
The builder thought about it. On the next project, he did it differently.
I came to the customer and asked:
— Why do you need a bridge? What will you transport across it? Who will use it? What time of year?
The client was surprised: no one had asked him about it before. He explained everything in detail.
The builder built a bridge that was perfect for the job. The client was delighted and recommended the builder to everyone he knew.
Ten years later, the builder became the most famous in the region. People lined up to see him.
"What's your secret?" my colleagues asked. "You're no better at building than anyone else."
“I build the same way,” the builder answered. “But before I build, I listen.”
Question:
What is more important: building quickly or building what the customer needs?
MAIN:
Don't build bridges that no one needs. First, find out where people want to cross.
________________


Chapter 2.59 The Tale of the Apprentice and the Master
(Study and copy from the masters)
A student came to the master and said:
— I want to become a great artist. Teach me.
The master gave him a brush and said:
— Go to a museum and copy a hundred paintings by great masters.
The student was indignant:
— How is this possible? I want something unique, my own, and you're forcing me to copy!
"Your own will come later," the master replied. "First, learn from those who already know how."
The student didn't listen. He left and started painting his own unique work. He painted for a year, two, three. Nobody liked his paintings.
He returned to the master, confessed, went to the museum, and copied a hundred paintings.
And then he suddenly realized: when he copied, he wasn't just repeating. He absorbed the techniques, understood the logic, and found his own.
A year later his paintings were already being bought.
Question:
Why did copying help the student become a master?
MAIN:
First, copy someone else's experience a hundred times. Then you'll find your own method.
Don't be afraid to learn from the masters. 
________________


Chapter 2.60 The Story of the Old Master and His Apprentice
(Be consistent)
An old master had an apprentice. The master did the same thing every day: he rose at 6 a.m., sharpened his knives, fired the furnace, forged metal until lunch, rested after lunch, then sharpened his tools, ate dinner, and went to bed.
The student looked at this and was bored:
"Master, this is boring! The same thing every day! Let's go to the mountains today, to the river tomorrow, the day after..."
“Go,” answered the master. “And I will work.”
A year later, the apprentice was still wandering the mountains and rivers. Meanwhile, the master had already opened his own forge, racked up orders, and become wealthy.
"How did you do that?" the student asked in surprise. "You were sitting still the whole time!"
"I wasn't sitting," the master replied. "I took a small step every day. You got nowhere in a year, but I took 365 steps that led me to my planned goals."
Question:
Who was right - the master who did the same thing, or the student who looked for variety?
MAIN:
Success isn't about heroism once a month. It's not about variety, but about repetition.
It's a daily routine. 365 small steps a year is already the pinnacle. 
________________


Chapter 2.61 The Tale of Three Gardens and One Wise Gardener
(Self-reproduction is the only way to Freedom)
Once upon a time there lived a wise gardener. His name was Grandfather Matvey.
He had four sons. When the sons grew up, Grandfather Matvey called them and said:
"You're all grown up. It's time for you to start your own business. Here are five gold coins each. That's your start. But there's one condition: the business must be related to apples and orchards. And which path to take—that's up to you."
The sons took the coins, thanked their father and went their separate ways.
---
The Story of the First Son. A Business Built by People
The first son, Ivan, was a Technologist. He loved to understand everything down to the last detail: how to plant properly, how to water, how to prune branches so that the tree would produce maximum fruit.
He spent his 5 coins on seedlings and went to the neighbors.
"I know how to grow the best garden," he said. "I'll give you seedlings and teach you the whole process. And you'll give me a small portion of the harvest."
The neighbors agreed. Ivan taught them every step: how to prepare the soil, how to care for the seedlings, how to remove weeds, how to water and fertilize the trees.
A year later, the first harvest was harvested. The neighbors saw the results and believed: the technology works.
"And now," said Ivan, "each of you can teach others. Go to your neighbors. Teach them everything I taught you. And I will receive a share from each new garden."
Thus began a chain of events. Ivan taught ten others. Ten others taught their own. Five years later, hundreds of people were growing gardens using Ivan's method. He no longer planted himself—he only taught and tested. This is self-reproduction: people teaching others.
This path is called"A business that people build".
---
The story of the second son. The place that grows itself.
The second son, Peter, was the Organizer. He didn't like digging, but he knew how to negotiate and unite people.
He spent his 5 coins on boards and tools and began building.
Right in the city center, he organized a large square with sunshades. He set up benches and went out to the people.
"Come here, sellers," he said. "Arrange your apples. And you, customers, come here and choose the best ones."
At first, there were few sellers. And few buyers. But Peter didn't give up.
When the number of vendors increased, buyers became more interested—there was more to choose from. And when there were more buyers, the vendors themselves flocked to the square. Where there are plenty of buyers, there are sales.
The square grew on its own. Vendors paid Peter coins for space. Each new vendor made the square more attractive to buyers. Each new buyer made it more attractive to vendors. This is self-reproduction: the more people there are, the more valuable the space becomes for everyone.
Within a few years, Peter's Square was the largest in the area. He didn't plant or harvest anything. He simply ensured the square was tidy. And coins flowed in like a river.
This path is called"A place that grows itself".
---
The Story of the Third Son: Money That Makes Money
The third son, Vasily, was the most patient. He didn't go to the neighbors like Ivan, and he didn't build a square like Peter.
He found the owner of a large, well-functioning garden. The garden was already producing a harvest. But the owner wanted to expand, and he didn't have the spare money to do so.
Vasily came and said:
"I have 5 gold coins. I'll give them to you for expansion. And you'll give me a portion of the profits from the new trees."
The owner agreed. The garden expanded. The harvest grew. Vasily received his first coins—and immediately gave half to another owner, in another garden. And then to another. And another.
Each time, he invested half of what he received. He didn't spend it on himself. He lent it out at interest. This is self-reproduction: money makes money.
Years passed. Coins from the Masters flowed to Vasily from all sides. He didn't plant. He didn't build. He didn't teach. He only made sure one thing: that half of it always grew.
This path is called"Money that makes money".
---
Three brothers. Three paths. One freedom.
Many years later, the brothers met again. All three were rich. All three were free. All three hardly worked—their businesses worked for them.
Ivan said: "My business is growing because people teach people. I've built a self-reproduction system for people."
Peter said, "My business is growing because every new person makes it more valuable for everyone. I've built a self-replicating system."
Vasily said, "And my business is growing because I didn't spend what I could have reinvested. I built a self-propagating money system."
---
The main secret that the fourth brother didn't notice
Grandfather Matvey's fourth son, Stepan, also received five gold coins. But he chose neither path.
He went and got a job as a hired hand for the owner of a large garden. He paid him a salary. Stepan worked from morning until night, received his honestly earned coins, and was happy.
He had no desire to strive for anything, teach anyone, or build anything. He simply lived, worked, received a guaranteed salary, and didn't worry about tomorrow.
"Why didn't you start your own business?" the brothers asked.
"Why?" Stepan shrugged. "I'm fine as I am. I work, I get paid. I don't need greatness. I need a quiet life."
And the brothers understood: this, too, was a choice. No worse and no better than their own. Just different. Stepan didn't have self-reproduction. But he did have what he valued more—peace.
Question:
Which path is closer to you?
Do you want to teach people, build a place where everyone will come, invest coins in other people's businesses - or just live quietly and work for a salary?
MAIN:
Three types of self-reproduction - three paths to freedom.
And one path is to a simple and calm life.
All four have a right to exist.
But only the first three build what grows without you.
________________


Chapter 2.61.1 The Story of the Old Well and the Man Who Dug It (Sector "I": Compound Interest Isn't a Freebie, It's a Deal with Time)
A man inherited an old house. The yard was overgrown with weeds, the fence was slanted, and water had to be carried from the river—three kilometers there and three back. He spent several hours every day doing this.
One day an old neighbor came to him, looked at the yard and said:
"There's water under your house. I know it for sure. My grandfather used to say: the aquifer here is deep. Dig a well, and you'll always have your own source."
The man took a shovel and began to dig.
He dug for a day – it was dry. He dug for a week – it was dry. He dug for a month – it was dry. The neighbors who went to the river laughed at him:
"Why are you torturing yourself? We go to the river, but at least we don't dig. There's water anyway, what more do you need?"
The man remained silent and continued digging.
Six months later, the first water appeared at the bottom of the pit—muddy, dirty, and sandy. Unfit for drinking. The man was upset: "All this work—and all for nothing?"
The old neighbor came in again, looked into the hole and said:
"Dig deeper. This is only the top layer. The water will become cleaner, and the spring will become stronger. But if you give up now, everything will collapse, and you'll have to start over."
The man sighed and continued digging. He learned how to reinforce the walls to prevent them from crumbling. He read books about where to look for water and how to distinguish a good spring from a bad one. He learned patience.
A year later, the water became clear. Two years later, the well was full enough for both him and his neighbors.
The neighbors who laughed at him grew old. Walking to the river became difficult for them. But the man had his own source. He no longer depended on the weather, the river floods, or the queue at the watering hole. He simply went to the well and drew water.
One day a young man came to him and asked:
— How did you manage? I want my own well, too, but I don’t know where to start.
The man replied:
"First, find the right spot. Don't just dig anywhere. Second, be prepared for the first few months, maybe even years, without water. Or it might be dirty. Third, don't listen to those who go to the river. They don't understand why you should dig when you can just go for a walk."
"What's the hardest part?" the guy asked.
"Don't give up halfway. When you dig for a month, two, six months, and there's still no water, you give up. But if you give up, the hole will collapse, and all your hard work will be wasted. But if you keep going, one day you'll see a clear spring gushing from the bottom."
Question:
Why do you think some people go to the river while others dig a well?
Who of them came out on top ten years later?
MAIN:
He who digs deep always has water. He who seeks the quick always goes to the river.
________________


Chapter 2.62 The Story of the Magical Forest and the Hardworking Dwarves (The Mathematics of Man-Hours)
In a magical forest lived gnomes. They mined precious stones in the mountains.
One dwarf named Knop worked alone. Every day he went into the mountains, chipped the rock with a pick, and washed sand in the stream. By evening, he'd find one or two pebbles. It was barely enough to feed himself.
Another dwarf, named Smarty, did something different. He found ten other dwarves and said:
"Let's work together. I'll teach you, show you the best spots, and you'll give me a small portion of your finds."
The dwarves agreed. Smarty taught them all the secrets. Now ten dwarves brought stones every day, and Smarty received his share from each one.
A year later, Smarty no longer worked on his own. He simply wandered the forest, found new gnomes, trained them, and they, too, began working for him.
A year later, there were already a hundred gnomes in the forest working for Smarty. Each brought home a pebble a day. Smarty sat in a clearing and rejoiced.
And Knop continued to dig the mountain alone and found two pebbles.
"How do you do that?" Knop asked. "You don't even work!"
"I'm working," replied Smart Guy. "I just work with people, not with my hands. Right now, I have so many people on my team that they can do as much in a day as you can in six months."
Knop thought for a moment. Then he went to look for his gnomes.
Question:
Who is smarter - the one who works alone or the one who creates a team?
MAIN:
One gnome - 2 pebbles. One hundred gnomes - 100 pebbles. Choose who you're with.
________________


Chapter 2.63 The Story of the Traffic King
(THE BIG SECRET. Traffic is king)
In a certain kingdom there lived a king. He loved his people very much and wanted them all to be happy.
The king built the best bakery in the world. The most delicious pies, the freshest buns, the most fragrant muffins. But... no one came. The bakery stood empty.
Then the king built the finest forge in the world. The sharpest swords, the strongest armor, the most comfortable horseshoes. But... no one came. The forge stood empty.
The king became sad. He did everything perfectly, but people didn't know it.
One day an old sage passed by. The king complained to him:
— I make the best things in the world, but no one comes to me!
The sage smiled and said:
"Your Majesty, imagine you've built a bridge across a river. The most beautiful, the most sturdy. But if no one wants to get to the other side, the bridge will stand empty. Your problem isn't that you build poorly. Your problem is that you don't create a flow."
The king thought.
— What is a flow?
Flow is people coming to you. It doesn't matter what you offer. If there's flow, there will be sales, leads, and money. If there's no flow, you can have the best product in the world and still be sitting in an empty room.
The king understood. He stopped building perfect things and began creating a flow. He called people, talked about himself, shared, and attracted them.
And soon people started coming to him. Lots of people. And then... then the math worked.
Of every 100 people who came, 20 became regular clients. Of those 20, 4 became his assistants. And those 4 did 80% of the work.
The king finally understood the main secret:Traffic cures everything.
Question:
What do you think is more important: doing things perfectly or having people come to you?
MAIN:
The best product without an input flow is just dust on the shelf.
Create a flow and mathematics will do its job. 
________________



SECTION 2 SUMMARY
Self-assessment checklist (in questions):
                                                                                                                                             * Do I understand what assets and liabilities are? (A story about two apartments)
                                                                                                                                             * Do I know my monthly expenses? (A story about Petrovich)
                                                                                                                                             * I understand who is the boss inside me? (A story about five brothers)
                                                                                                                                             * Am I building a system, not chasing goals? (A story about a conveyor belt)
                                                                                                                                             * Do I test ideas through small experiments? (A story about Aunt Lyuba)
                                                                                                                                             * I know that 20% of the effort gives 80% of the results? (The Pareto story)
                                                                                                                                             * Can I wait for the system to start working on its own? (The Snowball Story)
                                                                                                                                             * Am I taking small steps every day? (The Drop Story)
                                                                                                                                             * Do I use levers? (A story about two movers)
                                                                                                                                             * Am I removing excess? (The story of the gardener with scissors)
                                                                                                                                             * I remember that no one owes anyone anything? (a story about a debtor)
                                                                                                                                             * Do I take into account that people can make mistakes? (A story about a builder)
                                                                                                                                             * Looking for my 4%? (Pumpkin Patch Story)
                                                                                                                                             * Do I Have a Team? (A Story About Three Masters and a Cart)
                                                                                                                                             * Do I have all Five Faiths? (A balloon story)
                                                                                                                                             * Am I building a system where people create people? (A story about a garden)
MAIN:
Business math never lies. You just have to know the stories.
________________


________________


SECTION 3. COMMUNITIES
(Stories and parables about trust and friendship)
________________
Introduction to the section
The story of the old oak tree and the forest dwellers
In one forest lived an old oak tree. It was so large and spreading that all the animals could hide under its crown.
It was always fun under the oak tree. Squirrels played tag, hares told stories, birds sang. Animals came here not only to hide from the rain but also to see each other.
One day a traveler passed by and asked:
"Oak, why are there always so many people under you? The other trees are empty, but you have a celebration."
The oak thought and answered:
— Probably because I don’t just stand there. I provide shelter from the rain. I give acorns to the hungry. I rustle the leaves so it’s not boring. I just...there is for them.
The animals didn't come because the oak called them. They came because it felt good under it.
MAIN:
Don't invite people. Create a place where they feel good, and they will come.
________________


Chapter 3.1 The Story of Matvey the Shoemaker and the Granny with Sunflower Seeds (The Power of Community and the Golden Rule of 10,000)
In one town, there lived a shoemaker named Matvey. He was a skilled shoe repairman, but he didn't socialize with anyone. Customers came in rarely and never returned.
One day, a neighbor asked me to fix his shoe sole. Matvey did a good job and was inexpensive. A week later, the neighbor's friend came, then another. And a month later, the customers disappeared again.
The old master came and asked: "Do you know their names? Where do they work? Do they have children?" Matvey was surprised: "Why would I need this? I repair shoes."
— People don't come for shoes. They come to be noticed.
Matvey started asking names, remembering them, and showing interest in their lives. Clients started coming back. And then they started bringing friends. One brought ten—Matvey started fixing his shoes for free. Within a year, he had a waiting list.
And on the other side of townMy grandmother sold sunflower seeds near the metro. A glass cost 50 rubles. She sold about 30 glasses a day, earning 1,500 rubles. It was enough to live on, but not much.
My grandson, a programmer from Moscow, came to visit and said, "Granny, why do you only sell at the metro? You should sell online."
"Oh, grandson, I don't understand anything about this internet. I'd better get around to the metro."
The grandson created a page for his grandmother online. He started posting photos of sunflower seeds, writing posts, and making videos of his grandmother roasting them. At first, there were 10 subscribers—the grandson's friends. Then 50. Then 200. Then 1,000.
Grandma was surprised: “So, these people will buy from me?”
A year later, Grandma had 10,000 subscribers. She was no longer sitting by the metro. She was packaging sunflower seeds in beautiful bags and mailing them across the country.
The price rose to 300 rubles per bag. I was selling 100 bags a day.
The grandson calculated: “Bab, do you know that out of these 10,000 people, 400 will buy anything from you?”
— Why 400?
— Because 4% of 10,000 is 400.
Grandma didn't understand math, but she stopped going to the bank—the money just came into her card.
---
One day, Matvey the shoemaker and the granny with the sunflower seeds met at the market and got to talking.
Matvey said, "I get it: first, make one person feel good with you. He'll bring ten. Of those ten, four will become your support."
Grandma nodded: "And I realized: 10,000 people aren't just a crowd. There are always 400 of them who will buy everything from you. The main thing is to have those 10,000."
Question:
Why do you think Matvey the Shoemaker and the Granny with the Sunflower Seeds became successful? What did they do that others didn't?
MAIN:
First, make one person feel good with you.
He will bring ten. Of the ten, four will become your support.
And when you have 10,000 people, there will always be 400 among them who will buy everything from you.
________________


Chapter 3.2: A Story About Two Coffee Shops on the Same Street
(Two types of promotion)
There were two coffee shops on the same street, right across from each other.
The first coffee shop had a salesman named Grigory. Every morning, he'd run out into the street, grab passersby by the sleeves, and shout:
— Come in! We have the best coffee! The lowest prices! Discount today! Gift tomorrow!
People tried to break free, waving them off, but some went in just to get rid of them.
A girl named Anya worked at the second coffee shop. She never grabbed anyone. She simply set up a small table outside, poured coffee into tiny cups, and smiled at passersby.
"Try it if you want," she said. "Just like that, for free."
Some people walked by, some stopped, drank coffee, and then moved on. And some went inside to buy more.
At the end of the month, Grigory sold 500 cups of coffee, but he was hoarse, tired, and spent a ton of money on advertising flyers. Anya sold 300 cups, but sat calmly, smiling, and drinking tea.
"See, I sold more!" Grigory boasted.
"Wait," Anya replied. "We'll count them as clean."
We did the math. Grigory's profit is 100 rubles per cup, minus the advertising, minus the throat medicine, minus the torn jacket.
Total: 30,000. Anya charges 150 rubles per cup, no advertising.
Total - 45 thousand.
"But that's now," Anya added. "And those you grabbed by the sleeves won't come back to you. But they come to me again and again. And they bring their friends."
A year later, Anya had a line on the street, and Grigory closed his coffee shop.
Question:
Why didn't people want to return to Gregory?
MAIN:
A shout brings quick money. A smile brings lasting friends.
________________


Chapter 3.3 The Story of Aunt Zina and Her Pies
(Personal brand and target audience)
In one city, there were five pie stands at the train station. The pies were all the same—made from the same dough, with the same filling, and priced the same.
But there was always a line at one kiosk. Aunt Zina was standing there.
Aunt Zina didn't just sell pies. She told every customer:
— Careful, it's hot! Son, you look pale today, are you feeling ill? Daughter, where's your mother? Here's a pie, say hi!
She remembered who liked it with cabbage, who with potatoes, and who with jam. She knew the names of all the taxi drivers who stood at the station. She let people try new pies for free.
People weren't going for pies. People were going to see Aunt Zina.
The owners of other stalls were surprised:
"Why is there a line for her, and ours is empty? We make the same pies!"
They sent a secret shopper. He bought a pie from Aunt Zina and brought it to them. They tried it—it was exactly the same. The dough was the same, the filling the same.
Then they approached Aunt Zina and asked:
"Aunt Zina, what's your secret? You make the same pies as everyone else."
Aunt Zina smiled and replied:
— You have pies. And I have me. People don't come to eat pies. People come to be treated with attention.
Question:
Why did people choose Aunt Zina's if the pies were the same everywhere?
MAIN:
Everyone's pies are the same. There's only one Aunt Zina. Become Aunt Zina.
________________


Chapter 3.4 The Story of Masha and the Three Drawings
(The "3-message" method)
Masha wanted to make friends with the new boy in her class. His name was Dima. He was quiet and didn't socialize with anyone.
Masha approached him during recess and blurted out:
— Let's be friends! Let's go for a walk after school!
Dima looked at her, blushed and ran away.
Masha was upset. She went to her older sister. She was smart and went to college.
"Are you stupid or something?" said the sister. "Who does that? It should be done differently."
And she taught Masha the three-drawing method.
Figure one:
Masha drew a funny cat and placed it on Dima's desk. Unsigned. Dima smiled, looked at Masha, and hid the drawing in his backpack.
Drawing two (two days later):
Masha drew their classroom, where everyone was running around and Dima was sitting alone. She captioned it, "Bored all alone." Dima stared at the drawing for a long moment, then nodded.
Drawing three (another day later):
Masha drew a picture of the two of them playing ball and captioned it, "Maybe we should try this?"
Dima approached her himself and said:
— Let's go for a walk after classes.
Question:
Why did Dima agree after three drawings, but run away after the first time?
MAIN:
Words can be scary sometimes. But a picture can say more. 
*Three drawings helped Masha become friends with Dima. But for a friendship to become strong, one time is not enough. It takes time and many more meetings.*
________________


Chapter 3.5 The Story of Aunt Galya and Her Jam
(Six Touches in Community)
*You can become friends in just three meetings. But for someone to buy and become a regular customer, it takes more. At least six.*
Aunt Galya made the best jam in the village: raspberry, cherry, currant, wild berry.
She came to the fair, placed the jars on the counter, and waited. People passed by, unnoticed.
Aunt Galya was upset. She took the jar and went to the person next to her at the counter:
— Please try it. Tell me honestly, is it tasty or not?
The neighbor tried it. She licked her lips. She bought a jar.
The next day, Aunt Galya gave some to another neighbor to try. She bought some too.
A week later, Aunt Galya no longer let people taste it, but simply placed small saucers of jam on the counter. People came up, tried it, and bought it.
Within a month, there was a line of people waiting for her. Everyone knew: Aunt Galya made the most delicious jam because she let people try it.
“Why don’t you scream like the others?” they asked her.
"Why shout?" Aunt Galya asked in surprise. "Let them try. They'll try once, they'll try a second time, and the third time they'll ask for it themselves."
Question:
How many times do you have to let someone try something before they want to buy it?
MAIN:
Don't shout about the product. Let him try it once, twice, three times—and he'll ask for it himself.
________________


Chapter 3.6 The Story of Petya and the Soccer Ball
(Six handshakes within the community)
Petya dreamed of a soccer ball autographed by the famous footballer. But he lived far away, in another city, and Petya didn't know how to approach him.
He went to his coach:
— Uncle Sasha, help me. I want a ball with my autograph, but I don't know how.
"Oh, I know someone!" the coach said. "My friend works at the sports school where this footballer started out. I'll give you his phone number."
Petya called the coach's friend. He said:
"I don't know the footballer personally. But I know his first coach. I can give him his phone number."
Petya called the first coach. He answered:
— I'm out of town right now. But I have his number. Here you go.
Petya called the footballer. He was surprised, but listened and agreed to sign the ball.
“How did you get to me?” he asked.
“Through the coach, the coach’s friend, and the first coach,” answered Petya.
"Three people," the footballer smiled. "And they say in six. Good job, come."
Question:
How many people helped Petya get to the football player?
MAIN:
Any person on the planet is no more than six handshakes away.
Just start.
________________


Chapter 3.7 The Story of a Grandmother and Her Grandson
(Six Priorities + Five Golden Questions in the Community)
The grandmother wanted to buy her grandson a birthday present. She didn't have much money, but he wanted an expensive toy.
She came into the store, looked at the price, sighed and got ready to leave.
The seller, an elderly man, came up and asked:
— Who is the gift for?
“For her grandson,” the grandmother sighed.
- What does he love most?
— Running around with the boys in the yard. He doesn't even have a ball.
— What will happen if you buy him a ball instead of an expensive toy?
- Well... he'll be happy. He'll run off to play right away.
— And if you don’t buy it?
- He will continue to sit at home alone.
The seller was silent. Then he said:
— I have a good ball. Inexpensive. And I even got a whistle for free. Would you like to take it?
The grandmother took it. The grandson was happy. And the salesman realized: sometimes you don't have to sell what you came for. You have to sell what you really need.
Question:
What did grandma want to buy first, and what did she buy in the end?
MAIN:
Don't sell what they ask for. Find out what they really need.
________________


Chapter 3.8 The Story of Aunt Klava and Her Pies
(Content, Neural Networks, and Automation as Community Gathering Tools)
Aunt Klava baked the best pies in the area. But no one knew about them except the neighbors.
My granddaughter came from the city and says:
- Granny, let me help you. I'll make a website.
Aunt Klava was scared:
- Oh, don't, I don't understand anything about this internet of yours.
- I'll do everything, you just bake the pies.
The granddaughter took a photo of the pies, wrote about Aunt Klava, and posted them online. A week later, the first order arrived. A month later, ten. A year later, there was a month-long waiting list.
"How does it work?" Aunt Klava asked in surprise.
"It's very simple," the granddaughter replied. "You bake the pies, and I tell you about them. Together we are strong."
Question:
Who made Aunt Klava's pies famous?
MAIN:
A good product without a story about it is just food.
Tell about him and he will become a legend.
________________


Chapter 3.9 The Story of the Tailor and the Strange Order
(Feedback)
A customer came to a tailor and said:
— Make me a suit. Here's the fabric, here's the money.
The tailor made it. It was beautiful, expensive, made of good fabric. The customer came, looked at it, and cried.
"What's wrong?" the tailor asked in surprise.
"I didn't ask for myself. I asked for my grandfather. He's old and hunchbacked, and he needs a loose suit. But you made it as if it were for a young man."
The tailor thought for a moment. He did it differently for the next order.
"Who is the suit for?" he asked the new customer.
— For my son.
- What is he like?
- Tall, thin, likes bigger pockets.
The tailor made a suit with large pockets. The son was happy.
"How did you guess?" the customer asked in surprise.
- I didn't guess. I asked.
Question:
What is more important: to sew beautifully or to sew what is needed?
MAIN:
Don't guess. Ask and you'll do the right thing.
________________



SECTION 3 SUMMARY
Self-assessment checklist.
· I understand that community is the main asset of the 21st century?
· I know that 4% of people make 80% of the turnover?
· Do I have my own community (channel, blog, group)?
· My goal is 10,000 subscribers?
· Do I use both types of promotion - active and passive?
Am I building a personal brand? Do people know me as an expert?
· Do I use the "3 messages" method when meeting people?
· I understand that a minimum of six touches is required to make a sale?
· Do I use the six degrees of separation theory to find the right people?
· Do I know how to ask the right questions (6 priorities + 5 questions)?
· Do I use content and neural networks to grow my community?
· I ask clients not “do you like it?”, but “what annoys you?”
MAIN:
Community is your greatest asset. Build it, respect it, and nurture it.
And mathematics will do its job.

SECTION 4. LEVERS
(Stories and parables about how to strengthen yourself tenfold)
________________
Introduction to the section
The story of the loader and the wheelbarrow
There were two loaders working at the construction site. One was carrying bricks on his back, five at a time. By evening, he was exhausted.
The second one took a wheelbarrow, loaded it with fifty bricks, and rolled it with one hand.
"You're clever," said the first. "You work less."
"No," the second one replied. "I get tired just like that. It's just that I have wheels instead of a back. That's the lever."
MAIN:
Don't strain yourself. Find something to ride on and what lever to use.
________________


Chapter 4.1 The Story of Archimedes and His Stick (Levers)
In ancient times, there lived a scientist named Archimedes. He was intelligent, but physically weak—a weakling, really.
One day the king came to him and said:
"Archimedes, we need to move a huge stone. Our strongmen are straining themselves, but it won't budge. Help."
Archimedes approached, looked at the stone, thought for a moment. Then he took a long stick, slid it under the stone, pressed down on the other end—and the stone rolled.
The king was stunned:
- How did you do that? You're a weakling!
"Give me a fulcrum," said Archimedes, "and I will move the Earth. I simply applied a lever."
Question:
What helped Archimedes move the heavy stone – strength or intelligence?
MAIN:
Don't drag the stone yourself. Find a lever, and the stone will roll on its own.
________________


Chapter 4.2 The Story of the Mistress and Three Assistants
(Delegation)
Once upon a time there lived a woman. She had a large family: a husband, three children, a mother-in-law, a dog, and a cat.
Every morning she got up at 5 a.m., made breakfast, washed the dishes, cleaned, did laundry, ironed, went shopping, made lunch, washed the dishes, cleaned, did laundry, made dinner, washed the dishes. She went to bed at 12 p.m.
This went on for 10 years.
One day, she collapsed from exhaustion. While she was in the hospital, her husband, children, and mother-in-law took over her chores. Her husband cooked breakfast, the children washed the dishes, and her mother-in-law went shopping.
When the woman returned, the house was clean, everyone was full, no one had died.
“How did you do that?” she asked, surprised.
“Very simple,” the husband replied. “We just did what you did. Only together.”
And then the woman realized: all these years she could delegate tasks, but she did not trust.
Question:
Why was the woman so tired, even though she had a large family?
MAIN:
If you do everything yourself, you are simply the busiest woman in the world.
Delegate.
________________


Chapter 4.3 The Story of the Farmer and His Sons
(The Triangle of Successful Business)
The farmer had three sons.
The first one was strong, but lazy. He could move mountains, but only if forced.
The second one was smart, but weak. He calculated and thought of everything, but couldn't do anything himself.
The third was ordinary—not particularly strong or intelligent, but stubborn. If he took on a task, he saw it through.
The farmer thought about it and distributed them like this:
"You, the smart one, will figure out what to plant and when. You, the strong one, will plow the land and reap the harvest. And you, the stubborn one, will keep an eye on everything, making sure no one shirks and that the harvest is sold on time."
The sons were offended at first—they each wanted to be in charge. But after a year, they saw the results: the harvest had tripled, everyone was busy, and no one was overworking.
The neighbors were surprised:
— How do you do it? Here, it's every man for himself, but you have a system.
"Not a system," the farmer replied. "It's just that everyone does what they're best at. Together we're strong. Alone, we're three laggards."
Question:
What role would you like to take on - strong, smart or stubborn?
MAIN:
One strong man is simply a strong man.
A team of strong, smart and stubborn people is an empire.
________________


Chapter 4.4 The Story of a Football Team Without a Captain
(The Power of Teamwork)
The boys got together to play football. They formed two teams.
One team had a captain. He assigned players to attack, defend, and keep goal. He told players who would take penalties and corners.
The second team had no captain. Everyone wanted to be a striker; no one wanted to be in goal. Everyone was chasing the same ball, pushing and shouting.
The first half ended with a score of 5:0 in favor of the team with the captain.
During the break, the second team players sat down and thought. They chose a captain and assigned roles.
They tied the second half 2-2. They didn't win, but at least they didn't lose without a goal.
"Why did you start playing better?" they were asked.
"Because we have a captain now," they replied. "And everyone knows their place."
Question:
What changed in the second team after the break?
MAIN:
Even the most talented players without a captain are just a crowd on the field.
________________


Chapter 4.5 The Story of the Builder and the Five Faiths (Five Faiths)
One builder decided to build a bridge across a wide river.
He believed in himself: "I can handle it. I have enough strength and patience." And every morning he got to work.
He trusted the workshop that gave him the tools: "They're reliable, they won't let you down." And the hammer never broke, and the rope never snapped.
He believed in the drawings and calculations: "If everything is done according to plan, the bridge will stand." And he didn't miss a single step.
He believed in his team: "These people know how to work. I can rely on them." And everyone did their job—without arguments or hard feelings.
He believed the bridge was needed by people: "Children would cross it to school, farmers to market, travelers to neighboring villages." And when things got tough, he remembered who he was building it for.
The bridge was built. It still stands. People cross it every day.
If even one faith disappeared, nothing would work.
Question:
Which of the five faiths do you think is the most important? Or are they all equally important?
MAIN:
Five Faiths. Remove any one of them and you won't build anything.
________________


Chapter 4.6 The Story of the Baker and His Oven
(Tools: Content, Neural Networks and Automation)
Once upon a time there lived a baker. He baked bread in a small bakery. He got up at 3 a.m., kneaded the dough, stoked the oven, and baked the loaves. By 8 a.m. the bread was ready, and the baker was already legless.
He baked 20 loaves a day and sold them to neighbors. The money was enough to live on, but not much more.
One day his son, who was studying in the city, came to him and said:
— Dad, why do you only bake 20 loaves? You can bake more!
"Son," the baker replies, "I'm tired as it is. I just can't handle it anymore."
The son thought and said:
"Let's install three ovens instead of one. And hire two helpers. You'll knead the dough, they'll bake and sell."
The baker was scared:
— It's expensive! Buying stoves, paying people...
- And you count - the son did not lag behind.
We did the math. It turned out that if we bake 60 loaves a day, there'll be enough money for the ovens, the helpers, and even some left over.
They installed ovens and hired help. Within a month, the baker was baking 60 loaves, and he felt less tired—others were helping!
A year later, the baker already had five bakeries in different districts. He visited them, checking on them, and the money was flowing in like a river.
Question:
What do you think was more important for the baker: installing new ovens or learning to trust his assistants?
MAIN:
You can't do much with just your hands. To grow, you need other hands.
________________


Chapter 4.7 The Tale of Three Travelers and a Cart
(Competencies + Resources + Money)
Three travelers decided to go to a distant city.
The first was a jack of all trades. He knew the way, could count, could fix a wheel, cook dinner, and negotiate with a forester. He had a whole sack of skills. But he only had three copper coins. And he didn't even have a cart.
The second traveler came from a wealthy family. He had a bulging wallet. But he knew nothing: how to find his way, how to light a fire, how to fix a wheel. He had money, but what good was it without skill?
The third traveler had an excellent cart. Sturdy, fast, with spare tires. But he didn't know where to go, and he had almost no money.
Each one of them could not set out on his own.
So they teamed up. The first drove the cart and chose the route. The second paid for food and lodging. The third provided the cart and ensured its proper functioning.
Three days later they were in the city. They got there faster than if they had each gone alone.
Question:
Who do you think was the most important of the travelers?
Or would the cart not have made it without any of them?
MAIN:
For a cart to reach its destination, you need skills, money, and the cart itself.
One without the other stands still.
________________


Chapter 4.8 The Story of the Fisherman and the Three Boats (Diversification)
A fisherman fished in a single boat. Every morning he went out to sea and returned in the evening with a catch.
But one day the boat broke down. The fisherman was left without a catch. Without money. Without food.
I went to my neighbor to ask for advice. He had three boats. One was at sea, one was being repaired, and the third was in reserve.
"Why do you have three?" asked the fisherman.
"If one breaks, I have two more," the neighbor replied. "I'll never be left without a catch."
The fisherman thought about it. He repaired his boat, bought a second, then a third. And never again was he left without fish.
Question:
Why was the neighbor never left without a catch?
MAIN:
One boat, one risk. Three boats, three guarantees.
________________


Chapter 4.9 The Story of Aunt Vera and the Lottery Win
(Big money = big problems)
Aunt Vera worked as a cleaner at a school. She lived modestly but harmoniously: her daughter, her cat, and a ficus tree on the windowsill.
One day she bought a lottery ticket. And won a million!
At first, Aunt Vera jumped for joy. Then she ran to her daughter:
— I'll buy you a fur coat! A car! A new apartment!
And a week later the problems started.
First came "old friends" Aunt Vera hadn't seen in 100 years. One asked for help for an operation, another for repairs, and a third just because.
Then the bandits came. They said, "Aunt Vera, share." Aunt Vera was very scared and gave it away.
Then the tax office came: “Where’s the declaration?” I had to pay.
A month later, a distant relative Aunt Vera had never heard of showed up and demanded help.
By the end of the month, a million was down to a hundred thousand. Aunt Vera was sad. Her daughter was nagging her. The friends who had received the money disappeared.
Aunt Vera sat in the kitchen and thought:
"I wish I hadn't bought that ticket. We were living peacefully..."
Question:
What do you think Aunt Vera did wrong?
MAIN:
Easy money - hard life.
Hard money, easy life. Choose.
________________


Chapter 4.10 The Story of the Little Cook and Three Recipes
(Study and copy from the masters)
The little cook came to the chef and said:
— I want to become a great chef. Teach me.
The chef gave him three recipes and said:
— Go and cook these dishes exactly according to the recipe. Don't change a thing.
The cook was indignant:
— How is this possible? I want something unique, my own, and you're making me repeat it!
"You'll get yours later," the boss replied. "First, learn from those who already know how."
The cook didn't listen. He started inventing his own dishes. But they were tasteless. No one ate them.
He returned to the chef and confessed. He cooked three dishes according to the recipe. And then suddenly he realized: when he repeated it, he wasn't simply copying. He understood why one ingredient was added earlier, another later, why the heat was this way and not that way.
A year later, his dishes were already being praised.
Question:
Why did repeating other people's recipes help a little cook become a master?
MAIN:
First, repeat it a hundred times. Then you'll find your own. Don't be afraid to learn from the masters.
________________



SECTION 4 SUMMARY
Self-assessment checklist.
· Do I understand what levers are and what types there are?
· Do I delegate routine tasks?
· Am I building a team instead of trying to do everything myself?
· Do I have people who work for me?
· Am I leveraging team synergy?
· Do I have all Five Faiths?
· Do I use modern tools (content, AI, automation)?
· Am I developing all three components: competencies, resources, money?
· Am I diversifying risks?
· I understand that big money brings big problems?
· Do I study and copy successful strategies?
MAIN:
One gnome - 2 pebbles. One hundred gnomes - 300 pebbles. Find the levers.
