SECTION 5. SCALING
(Stories and parables about growth and development)
________________
Introduction to the section
The story of a shashlik maker and three grills
A shashlik maker grilled meat on a single grill. He served 20 people a night. He was tired, but his income was barely enough to survive.
The son said, "Dad, let's set up three grills and hire two helpers. You'll just marinate the meat and monitor the quality."
The kebab maker was scared: "It's a hassle! People might make mistakes, they might steal..." But he decided to give it a try.
A year later, he had three grills, two assistants, and a line of 60 people. The kebab maker himself only marinated the meat and counted the profits.
"How did you do that?" the neighbors asked.
— Stopped frying myself. Started frying through others.
MAIN:
One grill feeds one. Three grills feed the whole street.
________________


Chapter 5.1 The Story of the Gardener and His Garden
(Scaling)
The gardener had a small garden. Apple, pear, and cherry trees grew there. The gardener tended them, watered them, and pruned them. Every year he harvested the fruit.
One day a neighbor came to him and said:
— Why do you only have one garden? Plant more!
The gardener thought about it. He could plant it, of course. But where would he find the time? He was alone.
The gardener thought and decided: first I’ll train my assistants, then I’ll plant a new garden.
He hired three assistants, trained them, and within a year he had two gardens. Then two more. And then he no longer worked himself, but only watched others work.
Question:
What did the gardener do first - plant new gardens or find helpers?
MAIN:
Before you plant a new garden, find those who will care for it.
________________


Chapter 5.2. The Story of the Treasure Map and the Eight Kingdoms
(Hierarchy of business models)
In a distant land, there lived a boy named Vitya. He loved adventure and dreamed of finding real treasure.
One day, he found an old map in the attic. It depicted eight kingdoms.
“Grandpa,” asked Vitya, “what kind of kingdoms are these?”
Grandfather put on his glasses and began to tell:
"Look, grandson. There are eight kingdoms, and each one lives its own way."
Kingdom number eight is the simplest.There, people bake their own pies and sell them at the market. They work hard, but they don't earn much—they get so tired.
In the Seventh KingdomPeople have invented machines that make pies themselves. All you have to do is press buttons.
In the sixth kingdomPeople say: "I won't bake pies. I'll teach others to bake, and they'll share their pies with me."
In the fifth kingdomThey built a large tower where all the bakers would come with their pies, and people would come to choose the most delicious ones. The tower's owner would take a small piece from each pie.
In the fourth kingdomThey invented a magic shovel. Everyone who wanted to bake pies bought this shovel first because it made kneading the dough easier.
In the Third KingdomThey built a huge square where those who wanted to sell pies and those who wanted to buy them met. The square's owner charged each vendor a small coin for rent.
In the second kingdomOnce upon a time there lived a wizard. He built an entire city where people could do everything: buy pies, order them for delivery, watch movies about pies, listen to music about pies, and even learn how to bake them. People came to this city and lived there because it had everything they needed.
"And what kingdom is this?" Vitya asked, pointing to the very top of the map.
— There’s some strange place drawn here... no houses, no people, only rivers and bridges.
Grandfather smiled:
- And this, grandson, is the most important kingdom.Kingdom Number OneThere are no bakeries, no squares, no cities here. There are onlyrivers and bridges.
"Rivers and bridges?" Vitya asked in surprise. "What do they have to do with it?"
"You see," said the grandfather, "in all the other kingdoms, people bake pies, sell them, build cities. But to pay each other, they needmoneyAnd money must somehow flow from one to another. And also be stored somewhere.
That's what rivers and bridges are for. Rivers arepayment systems, through which money flows. And bridges arebanks and vaults, where money can be left for safekeeping.
Without this kingdom, no other kingdom can function. The baker baked a pie, sold it, received the money... but where does he put it? How does he transfer it to the flour supplier? That's where kingdom number one comes in.
"And who lives there?" asked Vitya.
"Those who built these rivers and bridges live there. They don't bake pies or build cities. They simply make sure the money flows properly. And for this, they take a small coin from everyone who crosses their bridges."
"Are they the richest?" asked Vitya.
“Not always,” answered the grandfather. “But they are the most.”independentBecause people might stop buying pies, cities might fall into ruin, fashion might fade. But money will always flow. And as long as it flows, kingdom number one gets its coin.
Question:
Do you know what “rivers and bridges” your money flows through?
Main:
Pies can go stale, cities can collapse.
And the rivers will flow forever. Build bridges. 
________________


Chapter 5.3. A Story of Rivers, Bridges, and Magic Purses
(Group 0 - Payment systems and capital storage facilities)
In a certain kingdom, there lived two brothers. The elder, Mikhail, was a baker. He baked the most delicious pies in the entire region. The younger, Ivan, spent his days sitting by the riverbank, gazing at the water.
"Why are you sitting around doing nothing?" his brother scolded him. "Go help me knead the dough! There's already a line of people waiting, I can't keep up!"
“I’m not sitting idle,” Ivan answered. “I’m thinking.”
"What are you thinking about here?" Michael wondered. "You have to work - that's all."
The years passed. Mikhail baked more and more pies, hired assistants, opened a second bakery, then a third. His money kept growing. But so did his worries.
Every evening he sat and counted:
"These helpers need to be paid. This flour supplier needs to have money transferred. This landlord needs to receive payment. And all through different banks, different wallets, different payment systems. Each time there are fees, each time there are delays, each time there's a risk that the money won't arrive or will get stuck."
And Ivan continued to sit on the shore.
One day Mikhail couldn’t take it anymore:
"Brother, you're already old, and you haven't achieved anything in life! I built a whole pie empire, and you're still staring at the water!"
Ivan smiled and said:
- Come on, I'll show you something.
They walked along the river. Ivan led his brother to the enormous dam he had built over the years.
"What is this?" Mikhail asked in surprise.
"This is my dam," Ivan replied. "Every boat that sails along this river pays me a small coin. So small, almost unnoticeable."
— And are there many such boats?
"Thousands a day. Fishing boats, merchant ships, passenger ships. They all sail along my river. And I collect a coin from each one."
Mikhail did the math in his head and gasped:
- But your income is greater than mine from all my bakeries!
"Yes," Ivan nodded. "And you know what the main difference is? If everyone in your town stopped buying pies tomorrow, you'd go broke. But if even one boat sails down my river tomorrow, I'll get my coin. The river always flows."
"But how did you do it?" asked Mikhail.
“I didn’t build pie empires,” Ivan replied. “I builtinfrastructureI didn't think about how to feed people, but about how to connect those who feed with those who are fed. I built bridges, paved roads, deepened riverbeds.
Mikhail paused. He suddenly realized that all these years his pie business had depended on banks and payment systems created by other people. And his brother had become one of those creating these systems.
“What should I do now?” he asked.
“Nothing,” Ivan replied. “Keep baking your pies. You do it better than anyone. Just know this: behind every business, behind every empire, behind every city, there’s someone who built it.”rivers and bridgesAnd if you want to be truly free, sooner or later you'll have to think not only about the pies, but also about the rivers your money flows through.
Question:
Are you building pie empires or rivers with bridges? Or do you even think about who controls the rivers your money flows down?
Main:
Pies may go stale. But the river flows forever.
Build what will always be needed.
________________
Chapter 5.4 The Story of Grandma's Pie Shop
(Group 7 - one-sided businesses)
Grandma baked pies. The most delicious in the area. With meat, cabbage, potatoes, and apples.
Every morning she got up at 4 a.m., kneaded dough, sculpted, and fried. By 8 a.m., she'd gone to the market and sold. By lunchtime, she'd sold everything, returned home, and collapsed.
This went on for 20 years.
The granddaughter, who came from the city, asked:
"Grandma, why don't you open your own bakery? Hire some helpers and just give out recipes."
"Oh, my dear," sighed Grandma. "Who can do it better than me? These are my pies, my secrets."
The granddaughter left. But the grandmother continued to get up at 4 a.m. until her old age.
A chain bakery opened across the street. The pies there weren't very good, but they were baked by hired workers, and the owner sat at home counting his money.
Grandma looked at this and didn’t understand: how is it that her pies are better, but she lives worse?
Question:
Why did the grandmother whose pies were better live worse than the owner of a chain bakery?
MAIN:
The pies may be the best, but if you bake them alone, you're just a tired grandma with great pies.
________________


Chapter 5.5 The Soda Machine Story
(Group 6 - Process automation)
There was a soda machine at the train station. It poured a glass for 5 rubles. People dropped in coins, and the machine poured.
The vending machine's owner came once a week, collected the proceeds, refilled the syrup, and checked that everything was working. The rest of the time, the machine operated on its own.
One day a man came up to him and said:
"Listen, why don't you sell the machine gun? I want a business like that too."
"Why do you need mine?" the owner asked in surprise. "Buy yourself a new one."
"A new one is expensive," the man sighed. "And yours is already ready."
The owner sold the machine. The guy parked it at another station. And he also started coming once a week to collect the money.
Then I bought a second machine, a third, a tenth.
Five years later, he had a whole fleet of vending machines spread across the city. He hired a driver to drive around and collect the proceeds. He himself stopped working altogether.
And the first owner was left with just one machine gun. Because he missed the main point: you can do more than just own a machine gun; you can create a system of machines.
Question:
Why did one man become rich, while another was left with only a machine gun?
MAIN:
One machine feeds. A hundred machines make you rich. Replicate it.
________________


Chapter 5.6 The Story of the Grandfather Who Sold Honey
(Group 5 - Affiliate Programs)
Grandpa kept an apiary. The honey was excellent—linden, buckwheat, propolis. But he sold poorly—who would go to his village to buy it?
The grandson came from the city, looked at his grandfather’s suffering and said:
"Grandpa, let me help you. I'll sell your honey in town. You give me the honey, I'll sell it, and we'll split the money."
Grandfather agreed - there was nothing to lose.
The grandson created a website, wrote about his grandfather, bees, and ecology. Orders started coming in.
Within a month, the grandson already had 10 such helpers—students who also wanted to earn some extra money. They bought honey from their grandfather wholesale and sold it in their neighborhoods.
Grandpa stopped thinking about sales. He simply harvested honey and delivered it to stores. His income increased tenfold.
And the grandson sat in the city and coordinated the entire network.
"Grandpa," he said one day, "you and I created a partnership program. You produce, they sell, I manage. Everyone does their part."
Question:
Who worked more - the grandfather or the grandson?
And who earned more?
MAIN:
You don't have to sell it yourself.
Find a hundred people who will sell for you. 
________________


Chapter 5.7 The Story of a Price Comparison Website
(Group 4 – affiliate centers/aggregators)
Two students couldn't decide where to buy a phone. One store was expensive, another was even more expensive, and a third was a complete rip-off.
They searched through a bunch of websites, compared prices, and finally found where it was cheaper.
And then one says:
— Listen, why doesn't anyone make a website where you can see the prices in all the stores at once?
“Let’s do it,” the second one answered.
We did it. Simple, on the fly. We collected links to all the tech stores, wrote down the prices, and posted them online.
Within a month, the site was seeing 100 visitors a day. Within a year, that number had increased to 10,000. Stores themselves began asking to be added to the list. The students charged them a small commission for each click.
"We're not selling anything," they said. "We're just showing you where to buy what."
Question:
How do students make money if they don't sell anything?
MAIN:
You don't have to sell a product. You can sell the convenience of choice.
________________


Chapter 5.8 The Gold Rush Shovel Story
(Group 3 - Tool bases)
In the mid-19th century, a gold rush broke out in America. Thousands of people abandoned their homes and rushed to California to pan for gold.
An enterprising businessman went with them. But he didn't pan for gold. He opened a shop and began selling shovels, pickaxes, helmets, and wheelbarrows.
The prospectors panned for gold, found a little, sometimes grew rich, more often not. And the businessman sat in his shop and sold them tools. And he grew rich steadily, without risk.
When the gold ran out, the prospectors went broke and left. The businessman, however, kept the money and went on to open a similar shop elsewhere.
Question:
Who earned more - those who looked for gold or those who sold shovels?
MAIN:
Don't look for gold. Sell shovels to those who do.
They come and go, but shovels are always needed.

Chapter 5.9: A Story About a Flea Market and Avito
(Group 2 - double-sided platforms)
Every city has a flea market—a place where people sell old things and others buy them.
The flea market organizer doesn't produce anything. They simply provide space: sellers come with their goods, and buyers come with money. The organizer then charges the sellers a small fee for renting the space.
One day, a smart person thought: “Why not create the same flea market on the Internet?”
That's how the classifieds site came into being. Millions of people were selling, millions were buying. And the site owner just sat there and calculated the commission.
No warehouses, no products, no on-staff salespeople. Just a platform where people do everything themselves.
Question:
What does the owner of the classifieds site sell?
MAIN:
Don't sell yourself. Create a place where others sell. And take your cut.

Chapter 5.9.1 A Tale of Two Bazaars and Magic Coins
(The Secret to the Explosive Growth of Double-Sided Platforms)
Two new markets opened in one city.
The first bazaar was very beautiful. Merchants laid out their wares, everything sparkled, and the scent of fresh baked goods and spices filled the air. But for some reason, there were few vendors, and almost no customers came.
The second market was simple, without any embellishments. But the owner told everyone who wanted to sell there: "For the first three months, I'll charge you the lowest possible price for your space. And I'll also show everyone how much the first vendor at this market—Uncle Vanya with his pies—made."
Uncle Vanya earned 10,000 rubles in his first month. The market owner wrote this on a board at the entrance. People saw it and thought, "If he can do it, why can't I?" Vendors flocked to him. And where there are plenty of vendors, there are also buyers—there's plenty to choose from.
A year later, the first market closed—empty and beautiful. And the second market grew into a whole market town. Because people go where they can make money.
Question:
Why do you think the sellers went to a simple market and not a beautiful one?
MAIN:
Beauty attracts. And earning money keeps you going. Make people see other people's money and want your own.

Chapter 5.9.2 A Tale of Two Gardeners
(Content for platform promotion: only money and nothing but money)
In a certain village there lived two gardeners. Both sold flower seedlings.
The first gardener went out to the road every morning, laid out the pots and shouted loudly:
— The most beautiful seedlings! The best flowers! The lowest prices! Come on in, you won't regret it!
People passed by, occasionally stopping, but rarely buying. There was a lot of shouting, but little trust.
The second gardener didn't shout. He set up a large sign by the road and wrote on it:
"Last month, 500 pots of seedlings were sold here. Here's a photo of my invoice."
And next to it he hung a photo of his garden beds - even, green, well-kept.
People came up to the stand, read, and were amazed. Then they asked themselves: "What kind of seedlings are these? How much do they cost? Can I see them?"
The salesman wasn't praising his product. He was showing off his results. And a line formed in front of him.
Question:
Why do you think people believed the second gardener, even though the first one shouted louder?
MAIN:
Don't shout about how great you are. Show what you've done.
The result speaks louder than words.

Chapter 5.10: A Story About a City I Didn't Want to Leave (Group 1 - Ecosystems)
In one country there was an unusual city.
At first, only a bakery was built there. The best in the area. People came for bread, but then they left—there was nothing else to do in the city.
Then a dairy shop opened next to the bakery. Bread and milk were bought together—conveniently.
Then they built a school. Children studied, and their parents waited for them in the tea shop across the street.
Then there was a market where you could buy everything: from shoes to dishes.
Then there was a pharmacy, a bank, a park with swings, and even a cinema.
In a few years, a person could be born in this city, go to its school, get married on its square, work in its workshop, be treated in its hospital, go to its park, pay with its coin - and never leave its walls.
The city has become a whole world. Why leave when everything you need is already here?
People came for one day and stayed for a lifetime.
Question:
Why do you think people didn’t want to leave such a city?
What made him special?
MAIN:
When everything you need is collected in one place, there is no need to leave.

Chapter 5.11 The Story of a Boy and a Hundred Entrances
(Group 2 - Bulletin Boards)
One boy walked to school every morning through dozens of entrances. And everywhere he saw the same thing: notes on doors. "Puppies for sale." "Buying a bicycle." "Renting an apartment." "Looking for a nanny."
Every month the janitors tore down the old ones and people hung up new ones.
The boy thought: “If so many people want to post ads, and so many people read them, that’s crazy demand.”
He sat down at his computer and created a simple website in a month. It was just a board where he could write: "Selling," "Buying," "Renting," "Looking for."
But there was one important thing: an ad on the website cost pennies. Not free, like on a door, but just a little. But it was seen by thousands of people, not just the residents of a single building.
People tried it. They liked it. Within a month, the site was receiving 50 ads a day. Within six months, 500. Within a year, 1,000.
The boy didn't buy or sell anything. He simply created a place where people could find each other. And he charged a small fee for it.
Question:
Why do you think people were willing to pay for ads on his website when they could put them up on their front doors for free?
MAIN:
Even a small fee works if you provide something the free version doesn't – citywide coverage.

Chapter 5.12 The Mall Story
(Group 2 - marketplaces)
The merchant built a large shopping center. Beautiful, with display windows and escalators.
The sellers come to him:
— Can we trade with you?
"You can," says the merchant. "But you have to pay for the space."
The sellers agreed. They laid out their wares and began selling. Customers came in, chose, and bought.
The merchant doesn't sell anything. He simply rents out space. And he has more money than all the vendors combined.
Question:
Who earns more - the sellers or the person who rents out space to them?
MAIN:
A shopping center owner doesn't sell goods. He sells retail space.

Chapter 5.13 The Story of the Magic Taxi
(Group 2 - Uber-like platforms)
In one city, people couldn't hail a taxi. They'd stand at the stop, waiting, but there were either no cars or they were occupied.
A smart person came along and created an app. Now any driver could turn on the app and see who needed a car. And any passenger could press a button and see where the nearest car was.
Drivers no longer had to wait for their paychecks once a month. They'd accept an order, complete it, and the money would be deposited into their cards immediately. They could spend it the next day. So drivers flocked to the app. And where there are a lot of drivers, there are no passengers waiting. Where there are no passengers waiting, there are even more of them.
Drivers started earning more because they drove empty less often. Passengers found cars faster. And the smart driver took a small commission on each ride.
"I don't do anything," he said. "I just connect those who need help with those who can help."
Question:
What does a smart person do to make money?
MAIN:
Connect those who seek with those who can give. And take your percentage.

Chapter 5.14 The Job Exchange Story
(Group 2 - employment platforms)
In one city, people could not find work, and employers could not find workers.
A smart man came along and opened a job exchange. Employers came and posted applications: carpenter needed, cook needed, driver needed. Workers came and posted resumes.
A smart man brought them together. Employers paid him for finding a worker. The workers got paid for free.
"Why do employers pay, but employees don't?" he was asked.
“Because the employer needs the employee much more than the employee needs the job,” the smart man answered.
Question:
Who pays to find each other?
MAIN:
The one who needs it most pays.

Chapter 5.15: The Freelance Exchange Story
(Group 2 - freelance exchanges)
Artists, programmers, and writers lived in a certain city. They knew how to make cool things, but they didn't know who to offer them to.
And in another city, there were people who needed drawings, websites, and texts. But they didn't know where to find good artists.
A smart man came along and created a website where craftsmen could post their work and customers could leave requests.
The craftsmen were happy to find work. The clients were happy to find the craftsmen. And the smart man took a small commission from each transaction.
Question:
Who is the main player in this story – the masters, the clients, or the person who created the website?
MAIN:
The boss is neither the one who works nor the one who pays.
The main one is the one who brings them together.

Chapter 5.16: The Currency Exchange Story
(Group 2 - financial platforms)
A traveler arrived in a foreign country. He had run out of money, but needed local currency.
He went to the market and found a man with a sign saying "Currency Exchange." He gave him some dollars and received local currency. It was slightly less than the exchange rate—but that was the exchange fee.
The man with the sign sat all day, exchanging money back and forth, making a little money on each transaction. By evening, he had a bag full of money.
He didn't produce or sell anything. He simply exchanged one currency for another.
Question:
How does a man with a sign make money?
MAIN:
Money can also be sold.
Bought cheaper, sold more expensive.

The artist drew beautiful pictures. He posted them online, people looked at them and enjoyed them, but it didn't bring him any money.
Then he found a website where he could sell his pictures. He uploaded them. Other people—designers, editors, advertisers—started buying them. Some for a ruble, some for a hundred rubles.
The artist received money, and the site took a small percentage.
"I don't do anything," the site's owner said. "I just give artists a place to find them."
Question:
How does a website owner make money if he doesn't draw anything?
MAIN:
Creativity should generate income.
Platforms help to do this.

Chapter 5.18 The Wheel of Fortune
(Group 2 - gaming and entertainment platforms)
One park has a huge Wheel of Fortune. Anyone who wants to spin it pays a coin and spins it. If it lands on a prize, you take it. If it lands on nothing, you get nothing.
People spun the wheels again and again. Some left with a toy, others empty-handed. But the park was always full.
The boy asked the Master of the Wheel:
— Aren’t you afraid that one day everyone will win and you’ll go broke?
The owner smiled:
— No. The wheel is designed so that ten out of a hundred people win. The other ninety just enjoy the game. I don't wonder whether I'll get lucky today or not. I just know the math. If the wheel is spun a thousand times a day, I always come out ahead.
The owner didn't play himself. He simply created a place where others played. And this place brought him coins every day. Rain or shine. Holidays or weekdays.
Question:
Why do you think the Wheel Master never worried about everyone winning? What was his secret?
MAIN:
The owner of the site doesn't play. He just knows math.

Chapter 5.19 The Delivery Service Story
(Group 2 - logistics and transport platforms)
Grandma baked some pies. She wants to send them to her grandson in another city. How can she do that?
I went to the post office. They said: pay, we'll ship it.
She paid. The post office took the pies, put them in a car, drove them to the station, loaded them onto a train, took them to another city, gave them to the postman, and the postman took them to his grandson.
The grandson is happy, the grandmother is happy. And the post office took money for it.
The post office doesn't produce anything. It simply moves things from one person to another.
Question:
What does the post office charge for?
MAIN:
Moving things and people will always be necessary.
Make this process convenient and earn money.

A boy and a girl lived in the same city. They lived next to each other, walked the same streets, but never met.
And in another city, a smart person created a dating site. A guy signed up, a girl signed up. The site connected them.
They met, fell in love, and got married.
Before they found each other, they paid a small amount of money each month to a website to feature information about them.
The smart man sat and rejoiced: he simply created a place where people can find each other.

What does a dating site sell?
MAIN:
People will always seek each other. Help them find each other—and earn money.

Chapter 5.21 The Story of the Best Chefs Association
(Interesting business models from Group 7)
The city's best chefs got together and decided to create an association. So everyone would know: if a chef is in this association, they're the best.
They began holding competitions, presenting awards, and accepting new members. Chefs paid dues to be part of the association. Restaurants paid to get their chefs noticed.
But the association's organizers didn't prepare anything. They simply said, "We know who's the best." And everyone believed them and paid them.
Question:
What are the association's organizers selling?
MAIN:
Status can also be sold. People are willing to pay for the title of best.

Chapter 5.22 The Story of Ilyich's Lamp
(The lamp you build yourself)
At the beginning of the 20th century, electricity was brought to villages in Russia. Light bulbs lit up in huts. People rejoiced—it was bright and cozy, and they didn't have to burn kerosene.
This lamp was called the "Ilyich lamp".
A hundred years have passed. The lamps still burn. And those who installed them are long dead.
One man looked at this and thought: "I wish I had a lamp like that. I could build it once, and it would shine forever."
And he began to build not a business, but a system. One that would work without him.
It took him a long time to build, ten years. But he did it. And now it was working, bringing in money, and he just had to make sure the wires didn't burn out.
Friends asked:
- Why aren’t you working?
“I’m working,” he replied. “I’m building something that will work even when I’m gone.”
Question:
What is more important: working yourself or building a system that will work without you?
MAIN:
Don't be a slave to your lamp. Build a system that will shine without you.
________________


Chapter 5.23 The Story of Two Travelers Who Went to the Same Mountain (The Way of the Warrior: One Norm, One Discipline, One Freedom)
In a distant land, there stood a high mountain. At its summit, people said, lay a spring that granted freedom. Whoever drank from it would never again be a slave—to money, to circumstances, to the dictates of others.
There were two paths leading to the mountain.
The first traveler.
The first traveler was a builder. He brought with him an axe, a saw, a hammer, a bag of nails, rolls of blueprints, and a thick book of construction laws.
"I'll build my own road," he said. "I'll choose the route myself, I'll lay the trail myself, I'll put up the signs myself. When I get there, the mountain will be mine."
He chopped down trees, removed rocks, and leveled the land. He built bridges across ravines and fixed railings on steep slopes. Sometimes other travelers would join him, and he taught them how to hold an axe, hammer nails, and read blueprints.
Years passed. The first traveler built a sturdy, beautiful road. But he rarely looked at the summit—he was always busy repairing, improving, and expanding. He became the Master of the Road. And the road became his home.
It took him 10 years to reach the top.
The second traveler.
The second traveler was a wanderer. He took only a staff and a small compass. He had no plans, no tools, no books.
"I'll follow the prepared path," he said. "Someone has already paved the way before me. I don't need to build—I just need to walk."
He found an old path. Not the most beautiful, not the widest, but it led in the same direction as the builder's path. The path had been trodden before him—by dozens, hundreds of travelers who had come before and ahead of him.
The second traveler set a rule for himself:
Every day – a thousand steps. No more and no less. Every day. Without fail.
He didn't think about how far it was to the summit. He didn't compare himself to those who walked faster. He simply took his thousand steps. Every day. Rain or shine. When it was easy and when his legs ached with fatigue.
After 3 years he reached the top.
He sat down by the spring, drank some water, and looked down. Far below, he saw the builder. He was still strengthening his road, adjusting the railings, hammering in new signs. His path was wider and more beautiful than the wanderer's. But the builder himself had made little progress toward the summit in three years.
---
Meeting at the source.
When the builder finally reached the top (this happened 10 years later), he saw a wanderer sitting by the spring and calmly watching the sunset.
"How did you get here faster?" the builder asked. "You had no maps, no tools, no crew."
"I carried less," the wanderer replied. "You carried the road. I carried only myself."
"But the road will remain!" the builder objected. "Others will use it. And your path will become overgrown."
"Perhaps," the wanderer nodded. "But I didn't build the path so it would remain. I walked along it to reach the summit."
They were silent.
"Your path is harder than it seems," the builder said. "Doing the same thing every day, without a team, without plans, without confidence that you're even going the right way... I couldn't do it."
"And your path is harder than it seems," the wanderer replied. "Being responsible for everyone who follows you. Fixing what breaks. Answering when someone goes astray. I couldn't do it."
What is this story about?
They both reached the summit. Just by different roads.
The first traveler built his own roadHe carried tools and blueprints, taught others, and took on responsibility. He became the Master—but the journey took 10 years. But he left behind a path for others to follow.
The second traveler followed the prepared pathHe carried only a staff and a compass, taught no one, and was responsible only for himself. He met his quota every day—without failing. He remained a Wanderer—and reached the destination in three years. After him, there was no road left. Only his example.
Question:
Do you already know which road is yours?
If you want to build, manage, take responsibility and leave behind something that will outlive you, choose the path of the first traveler.
It's longer, it's heavier, but you will become the Master.
If you want simplicity, discipline, and freedom, choose the second path. It doesn't require talent, connections, or start-up capital. It requires only one thing:Do your quota every day. And don't stray from the path.
MAIN:
One mountain. Two paths. Everyone chooses their own. The main thing is to keep going. 
________________


Chapter 5.24 The Story of the Main Secret
(THE BIG SECRET. Traffic is king)
Once upon a time there was a boy who wanted to become a great builder. He studied with the best masters, studied all the blueprints, and knew all the secrets of construction.
But one day he met an old man who built the largest city in the kingdom. The boy asked:
"Grandpa, what's your biggest secret? Do you know any special blueprints? Do you have any magical tools?"
The old man smiled and said:
- Come on, I'll show you my city.
They set off. The boy looked around and saw: there was a bakery—they didn't make the best pies, but there was always a line. There was a blacksmith—they didn't forge the sharpest swords, but knights were always coming in. There was a school—the teachers weren't the best, but new students came every year.
"Grandpa," the boy was surprised, "everything about you is average, but there are people everywhere! How is that possible?"
“Now look at my neighbor,” said the old man.
The boy looked at the neighboring town. It had perfect bakeries, perfect forges, perfect schools. But... it was empty. People didn't go there.
"You see," said the old man, "you can have a perfect product and sit in an empty room. Or you can have an average product but a powerful flow of people—and still be king."
- But how to create this flow?
"There are four steps," the old man replied. "And they are suitable for everyone: for those who act independently and for those who build a system."
Step One: Create Content.
Text, video, images—anything that attracts people's attention. If you want, do it yourself; modern tools make it easy. If you want, hire specialists. The main thing is to have content.
Step two. Launch Traffic.
A stream of people seeing your content. Choose a platform. Be consistent. Talk to your audience. Doing it yourself is good. Hiring a traffic manager is even better. What matters is the result: people are coming.
Step three. Choose your Path.
Affiliate programs offer a quick start and low risk. Your own platform offers a long road and greater freedom. Traffic is essential in both cases. And who generates it—you or your team—is up to you.
Step Four: Reach Critical Mass.
When your system gets big enough, it starts to grow. Clients bring clients. Members bring members. You don't spin the wheel anymore—it spins itself. You just monitor the numbers and set the direction.
The boy thought:
- How much time does it take?
“Two or three years,” the old man replied. “If you take steps every day.”
— What will happen in three years?
"And in three years, you'll have a system that runs itself. You'll be able to choose: build on it or simply reap the benefits. The main thing is to get started."
Question:
Which path will you choose: a quick start or a long empire-building journey?
MAIN:
Traffic cures everything.
Drive traffic and build your freedom.
________________


 
SECTION 5 SUMMARY: SCALING
Self-assessment checklist.
· Do I know what the eight kingdoms are?
· Do I remember which kingdom is the simplest and which is the most difficult?
· Did I learn the main lesson from the story about the three doors?
· I understand why all kingdoms are important, just to each his own?
· I remembered why rivers flow forever, even when pies go stale?
· Do I know which kingdom I am in now?
· Do I know which kingdom I want to be in in 3 years?
· And in 10 years?
Three main lessons from stories:
                                                                                                                                             1. First, grow where you already grow. Then go to new places.
                                                                                                                                             2. All kingdoms are important. Everyone just chooses their own.
                                                                                                                                             3. Pies can go stale, cities can collapse. But rivers flow forever.
Final thought.
Even if you're in the simplest kingdom right now and just baking pies, that's okay. The main thing is to know that there are other kingdoms. And that one day you can build your own city. And maybe even a river that will carry the world's money.
MAIN:
It doesn't matter which kingdom you are in today.
What matters is what you want to be in tomorrow.
You can always move and change directions. And at any time.
________________


________________


SECTION 6. STEP-BY-STEP ALGORITHM
(Stories and parables about how to achieve your dreams)
________________
Introduction to the section
The Story of the Map and the Magic Shoes
Once upon a time there was a boy who dreamed of finding treasure. He had an old map with a drawing of where the treasure was buried. But the map was so old that the boy didn't know where to begin.
He went to the old sage and asked:
"Grandpa, I have a map, but I don't know how to get there. What should I do?"
The sage smiled and gave him the magic shoes.
"Here are your shoes," he said. "They will keep you on track. But remember: shoes only show the way. You'll have to walk on your own."
The boy put on his shoes and set out. He walked for a day, then another, then a third. Sometimes he felt like he was going the wrong way. Sometimes he wanted to turn back. But his shoes led him forward, step by step.
A year later he reached the treasure.
"Thank you, Grandpa, for the magic shoes!" he said when he returned.
"These aren't my magic shoes," the sage replied. "You walked the entire path yourself. The shoes only helped you stay on track."
MAIN:
You have the map. You have the shoes. Now it's up to you.
________________


 Chapter 6.0: The Story of a Gardener Planning His Garden (The Global Plan: How to Build a Life According to KON-TON-FON)
One gardener decided to plant a large garden. Not just a few trees, but an entire orchard, complete with apple trees, pear trees, cherry trees, berry patches, and even an apiary.
He didn't grab a shovel right away. First, he sat down and drew a plan on paper.
— Here will be the apple tree plot. Here, the pear tree. Here, the vegetable beds. And here, the beehives.
Then he thought: “What do I need for this?”
— For apple trees, you need seedlings and tools. For pear trees, you need other seedlings. For an apiary, you need hives and bees. And all of this requires money and time.
Then he asked himself: “What should I do every day to make the garden grow?”
— Water. Remove weeds. Check for pests. Take care of the bees.
He wrote it all down. It turned into a long list of daily tasks. Not heroic ones. Ordinary ones.
And then he began.
Every day he did his quota. He didn't rush. He didn't give up. After a month, he checked: what was growing, what was lagging? After three months, he checked again. After six months, he checked again. After a year, he checked again.
And after two years the garden blossomed.
The neighbor asked:
"How did you manage? I also have soil, and seedlings, and water. But nothing grew for me."
The gardener replied:
— You didn't have a plan. But I did. A plan is when you know what to do today, tomorrow, and in a year.
Question:
Do you have a plan? Do you know what you'll do tomorrow, next month, next year?
MAIN:
A garden doesn't grow overnight. You need a plan, you need daily actions, and you need checkpoints to make sure everything is going well. The secret to success isn't heroism, but methodical adherence to a plan.
________________


BLOCK 1. PREPARATION (Week 1)
________________
Step 1. The story of Vitya and his dreams (Self-diagnosis)
Once upon a time there lived Vitya. He worked at a factory, lived like everyone else, but dreamed of a house by the lake. In the evenings, he'd watch videos about travelers and think, "I wish I had something like that!"
Years passed, but the house by the lake never appeared.
One day, he met a childhood friend. He'd once been just as ordinary, but now he drove a nice car and talked about his travels.
"How did it work out for you?" Vitya asked.
"It's very simple," my friend replied. "I didn't just dream about it. I decided I would do it. For three years, I worked like a horse, learned, made mistakes, but kept going. And now I'm reaping the rewards."
Vitya realized that all this time he hadn't been dreaming—he had simply wanted it. And wanting it and being willing to do it are two different things.
MAIN:
Dreams without action are just wishes. The willingness to do is where the power lies.
________________
Step 2. The story of Petrovich and his register
(Financial audit)
Petrovich was always complaining about having no money. His daughter, a student, came home for the holidays and said:
- Dad, let me write down all your expenses for the month.
Petrovich refused, but his daughter insisted. For a whole month, she wrote down everything: sunflower seeds, beer, chips, coffee at the kiosk.
At the end of the month she sat down and counted.
- Dad, do you know how much you spend on little things?
“Well, a thousand, probably,” Petrovich shrugged.
"Eight and a half thousand," said the daughter. "Which you didn't even notice."
Petrovich was stunned. He realized that you can't reach your goal if you don't know where you're starting from.
MAIN:
You can't get where you're going if you don't know where you're starting from.
________________


Step 3. A Tale of Two Traders (Choosing a Business Model)
Two merchants were selling at the market.
The first one sold other people's toys. He borrowed from a merchant, sold them, and received a percentage. There was enough money, but he couldn't make much—the percentage was small.
The second one made toys himself. It was difficult at first—there weren't many customers. But after a year, word got out, his toys became popular, and he became rich.
The first one was jealous, but didn't change anything. He was afraid to start his own.
MAIN:
Alien - fast and easy, but at a short distance.
Yours - long and hard, but forever.
________________


BLOCK 2. LAUNCH (Month 1-3)

________________
Step 4. The Story of Aunt Lyuba and Her Cheesecakes (MVP)
Aunt Lyuba dreamed of her own bakery. She wanted a big one right away, with a sign and tiled floors.
Nephew Seryozha said:
"Aunt Lyuba, let's try this first. We'll bake 20 vatrushki and sell them at the market."
"Who would I sell it to?" Aunt Lyuba said, offended. "I'll open a bakery, then I'll sell it."
But Seryozha insisted. On Saturday, they baked some vatrushki (cheesecakes) and went to the market. By lunchtime, they'd sold everything!
A month later, Aunt Lyuba had a permanent place at the market, and a year later, her own small bakery.
“It’s good,” laughed Seryozha, “that we didn’t start building a bakery right away, but checked it out first.”
MAIN:
Before you build a bakery, sell at least one cheesecake from the stand.
________________
Step 5. The story of Vovochka and the three letters
(First clients / 3-message method)
Vovochka fell in love with Masha and immediately blurted out: "Let's be friends!" Masha was offended and left.
His older brother taught him the three-letter method.
First, Vovochka wrote a note: "Masha, you answered so well in class today!" Masha replied, "Thank you."
Three days later: "Masha, help me solve this problem, you're smart." Masha helped.
A couple of days later: "Thanks for your help! Listen, maybe we should go to the movies?" Masha agreed.
"Why did you refuse after the first time, but agree after the third?" asked Vovochka.
“Because first you need to make friends,” the brother answered.
MAIN:
Make friends first, then propose. 
________________


Step 6. The story of the tailor and the strange order
(Feedback)
A customer came to a tailor and said, "Make me a suit." The tailor made a beautiful suit. The customer looked at it and burst into tears.
"What's wrong?" the tailor asked in surprise.
"I asked for it for my grandfather. He's old and hunchbacked. You made it for a young man."
The tailor thought for a moment. He did it differently for the next order.
"Who is the suit for?" he asked the new customer.
— For my son. He's tall, thin, and likes bigger pockets.
The tailor made a suit with large pockets. The son was happy.
"How did you guess?" the customer asked in surprise.
- I didn't guess. I asked.
MAIN:
Don't guess. Ask and you'll do the right thing.

BLOCK 3. SYSTEM (Month 6-12) 

________________
Step 7. The story of three builders (CON-TON-FON)
*Do you remember the three builders fromChapter 2.14?*
FirstI carried bricks myself, built one floor for a year and got exhausted.
SecondI spent a month looking for helpers, then built a house in six months.
ThirdHe spent so long drawing up the perfect plan that he never started construction.
You did the right thing.secondFirst, I assembled a team, then I built it. And then, when the house was ready, I set up the processes and started earning income without working manually.
That's itCON—TON—BACKGROUND:
                                                                                                                                             * HORSE— assemble a team and build a system (like a second builder who found helpers).
                                                                                                                                             * TONE— set up marketing, attract clients, so that the system works.
                                                                                                                                             * BACKGROUND— remove yourself from the operating system and get results.
If you missed this story or want to reread it in full, go back toChapters 2.14And here just rememberMain: First the command, then the settings, then the result.
________________
Step 8. The Story of the Old Shoemaker (Community)
The shoemaker sat in his workshop, not talking to anyone. People walked by, but no one knew about him.
One day, a neighbor asked me to have his shoe sole fixed. The shoemaker did a good job and was inexpensive.
A week later, a neighbor's friend came. Then another one.
"How did you hear about me?" the shoemaker asked in surprise.
— My neighbor told me. He says he has golden hands.
Soon the shoemaker had a queue, and he hired an apprentice.
MAIN:
Get one person to believe you. They will bring ten.
________________


Step 9. The story of the water wheel
(Closed loop of the money chain)
A miller had a running water mill, a turning wheel, and a milling operation. The flour was sold, and the proceeds were used to repair the mill's wheel so it would keep turning.
The neighbor's wheel broke because he drank away all his money and didn't fix it.
MAIN:
Always use part of what you earn to keep the wheel turning.
________________
UNIT 4. SCALING (Years 2-3)

________________
Step 10. The Story of Archimedes and His Stick (Levers)
The king asked Archimedes to move a huge stone. The strongmen puffed and panted, but the stone wouldn't budge. Archimedes slipped a long stick under the stone, pressed down, and the stone rolled.
- How did you do that? You're a weakling!
"Give me a fulcrum," said Archimedes, "and I will move the Earth. I simply applied a lever."
MAIN:
Don't drag the stone yourself. Find a lever.
________________


Step 11. The story of three doors
(Three Paths to Growth / Three Types of Scaling)
An apple merchant wanted to grow. Three doors faced him.
The first one led to the market square to set up more stalls.
The second one led to a new city where no one had seen apples.
The third one led to a garden where pears grew – you could start selling pears too.
The merchant first went through the first door, then the second, and then the third.
MAIN:
First, grow where you already grow. Then go to new places.
________________


Step 12. The Story of the Fisherman and the Three Boats (Diversification)
A fisherman was fishing in a single boat. One day, the boat broke down, and he was left without a catch.
The neighbor said:
"I have three boats. If one breaks, I have two more."
The fisherman thought about it and bought two more boats. And he never ran out of fish again.
MAIN:
One boat, one risk. Three boats, three guarantees.
________________


Step 13. The Finish Line Story (Exit Point)
The runner was running a marathon. He'd trained for years, covered hundreds of kilometers. And now he could see the finish line. It was so close. Just a little more, and he'd be the champion.
But suddenly he stopped.
"What's wrong?" the spectators shouted. "Run!"
“I know,” the runner replied. “I just want to remember this moment. I’ve been working towards this for so many years. I don’t want to pass it by.”
He stood for a second, smiled and crossed the finish line.
MAIN:
It's not just important to get there. It's important to notice that you've gotten there. 
________________


BLOCK 5. SUPPORT (Continuous)

________________
Step 14. The story of the builder and his assistants
(Human factor)
The builder got angry at his assistants when they made mistakes or were sick.
The old master said to him:
"Why are you angry? People aren't robots. They get sick and tired. Did you take that into account in your plans?"
“No,” the builder was taken aback.
"That's why everything falls apart for you. You should always leave some extra."
MAIN:
There are no perfect people. There are plans that have some room for error.
________________


Step 15. A Tale of Two Gardeners (Numbers and Metrics)
The first gardener counted how many apples he'd picked that day. The second looked at how many apples had grown and how much money he'd gotten for them.
At the end of the year, the first one had harvested a lot of apples, but half of them had rotted because he hadn't managed to sell them. The second one harvested fewer, but sold them all and earned more.
MAIN:
It's not enough to just do something. You have to do something that produces results.
________________


Step 16. The Juggler's Tale (Life Balance)
The juggler entered the arena. He held balls in his hands. He tossed them one after another. The balls flew, and no one fell. The spectators applauded.
Then they gave him a fourth ball. He tossed it too. Fifth, sixth, seventh...
And suddenly all the balls fell to the floor.
"What happened?" the audience asked.
"I forgot I only have two hands," the juggler replied. "I can't hold everything at once."
MAIN:
Life is like juggling: if you grab onto everything, you'll drop everything.
________________


Chapter 6.1 The story of Marina, who did not give up
(Case "Marina")
Once upon a time there lived Marina. She worked as an accountant, counting other people's money, but her own was never enough. Every evening, she dreamed of freedom.
One day she decided: enough dreaming, time to do.
At first, she simply calculated how much money she spent on living. It turned out to be 50 thousand.
Then I started a channel about personal finance. I wrote simple posts.
It was difficult at first, but she didn't give up.
A year later she had 5,000 subscribers.
Two years later - 15,000. She quit her job and started traveling.
"How did you do it?" they asked her.
"I just took a small step every day. And three years later, I found myself where I dreamed I would be."
MAIN:
There are thousands of Marinas like you. Why can't you be next?
