VOLUME 2. ENVIRONMENT AROUND

________________
________________


SECTION 7. THE STRUCTURE OF REALITY
(Stories about how the world works)
________________
Introduction to the section
The story of the little mouse and the big box
Once upon a time there lived a little mouse. He was born in a big, beautiful box where he was warm, well-fed, and safe.
He thought the whole world was his box.
One day the lid opened slightly, and the mouse looked out. There was a huge space there. A room. A house. A street. A forest. Mountains. The sky.
"What is this?" the little mouse asked the old mouse.
"It's the world," she replied. "It's much bigger than your box."
— Can I go there?
— You can. But first, study it. There are cats there, there are mousetraps, there is cold and hunger. But there is also cheese, and sun, and freedom.
The mouse thought for a moment. Leaving the box was scary. But staying meant never knowing what was out there.
MAIN:
The world is bigger than your box. Explore it and you will survive.
________________
Chapter 7.1 The story of Vitya, who lived in two worlds
(Microcosm and Macrocosm)
Once upon a time there lived Vitya. On the surface, he was an ordinary man. But something was constantly happening inside him.
He'd wake up in the morning and think, "Back to work. I'm sick of it all. My boss is stupid, my salary is low, my colleagues are driving me crazy." This was his inner world—a world of thoughts and feelings.
Then he'd go outside, get on a bus, go to the factory, work, and return home. This was the outside world—the world of events and circumstances.
One day, something strange happened. They announced a bonus at the factory. Vitya was overjoyed and ran home happy. His wife greeted him with the news: she was being laid off.
Vitya immediately forgot about the bonus and became sad.
“What happened?” his friend asked.
“Life is pain,” Vitya answered.
The friend looked at him and said:
"Vitya, don't confuse things. You had two things going on today: a bonus and your wife's problems. A bonus is good. Problems are uncertain. But you chose to dwell on the bad. Your inner world decided that the good is unimportant, and the bad is a disaster. That's why you're suffering."
Vitya thought about it. It's true. Events are one thing, but thoughts are another.
Question:
What does our mood depend on – events or how we look at them?
MAIN:
The outside world influences you. Your inner world determines how you react to it.
________________
Chapter 7.2 The Story of the Matryoshka Doll and Its Secrets
(Matryoshka principle)
A mother bought her daughter a matryoshka doll. A beautiful, painted one, big.
My daughter opened a matryoshka doll, and inside was another, smaller one. She opened the second one, and there was a third. The third, and a fourth. And so on down to the smallest one, the size of a pea.
"Mom, why are they all inside each other?" the girl asked.
"That's how the world works," Mom replied. "You're inside a family, the family is inside a house, the house is inside a city, the city is inside a country, the country is inside a planet, the planet is inside a universe."
"Am I the most important?" the girl asked.
"You're the smallest," Mom smiled. "But without you, there wouldn't be any of the others. Every matryoshka is important."
The girl thought for a moment. Then she asked:
— Can I influence the country?
"Very little," Mom replied. "Only through elections. But the country influences you every day—through taxes, laws, roads."
— And what about the universe?
— Nothing. But the universe is upon you—with sun, rain, wind.
The girl realized: the bigger the matryoshka doll, the less it influences her. But the more that doll influences her.
Question:
Which nesting dolls can you influence, and which ones influence you?
MAIN:
You are the smallest matryoshka doll.
Take the big ones into account, but don't try to change them.
________________
Chapter 7.3 The Story of the Fish Tank
(Who lives in these worlds)
Fish lived in one aquarium. Big and small, golden and silver, fast and slow.
Among them were champions - three goldfish who always grabbed the food first, swam the fastest and took the best places.
There were the average fish—mostly silver ones. They swam around, ate whatever was left, and kept a low profile.
There were stragglers - a few pale fish that were always hiding in the corners and never made it to the food.
The owner of the aquarium looked at them and thought: “I wonder if it’s the same in the human world?”
It turned out to be the same. Every city, every company, every family has its champions, its average performers, and its laggards.
And everyone can change their role. Today you're a champion at work, tomorrow a laggard at the gym, the day after tomorrow a loser at the family meeting.
Question:
Who are you today - a champion, a middling team or a laggard?
MAIN:
Each aquarium has its own hierarchy.
You can be a champion in one thing and a laggard in another.
________________


Chapter 7.4 The Story of School Rules
(Laws of Aquariums)
One school had strict rules: don't be late, don't run in the hallways, don't use phones, and you have to wear a uniform.
One student decided the rules were nonsense. He was late, running around, on his phone, and didn't wear uniform.
He was called to the principal's office, scolded, given bad marks for his behavior, and his parents were called.
"Why are you picking on me?" the student protested. "I'm not doing anything wrong!"
"You're breaking the rules," the principal replied. "And rules are there to be followed. If you don't like the rules, go to another school."
The student transferred to another school. The rules there were different—you could wear whatever you wanted, but you couldn't skip classes. He skipped classes—and was expelled.
Then he entered college. There were almost no rules there—just pass your exams on time. He failed—and was expelled.
Then he got a job. There were written and unwritten rules. He didn't follow them, and he got fired.
"Why do I always have bad luck?" he complained to his friend.
"Because you don't want to understand one simple thing," my friend replied. "There are rules everywhere. If you want to be here, follow them. If you don't want to follow them, go somewhere else. But there will be rules there, too."
Question:
Is it possible to live without rules at all?
MAIN:
There are rules everywhere.
If you want to play, play. If you don't, find another game.
________________


Chapter 7.5 The Story of Vanya, Who Thought He Was Making His Choices (The Illusion of Choice)
Vanya considered himself a free man. He chose what he ate, what he wore, where he lived, and who he befriended.
One day, a grandfather from another country came to visit him. He looked at Vanya's life and asked:
- Why do you speak Russian?
“Well... because I’m Russian,” Vanya answered.
— Did you choose to be Russian?
- No, I was born.
- Why do you live in this city?
— My parents live here.
— Why do you believe in God (or don’t you believe)?
— That's what my parents taught me.
— Why did you become an engineer?
— Everyone in our family is an engineer.
Grandfather sighed:
"Vanya, you think you're free. But in reality, 90% of your life was chosen for you before you could even speak."
Vanya thought about it. It's true. Language, country, city, religion, profession—all of this was given to him. He didn't choose any of it.
"What can I choose myself?" he asked.
"Everything else," Grandpa replied. "But to do that, you first need to understand what you were given and what you chose yourself."
And if you don’t like something that was given to you, you can change it.
Question:
What in your life did you choose, and what was chosen for you?
MAIN:
You didn't choose where you were born. But you can choose who you become.
________________


Chapter 7.6 The Story of Four Doors Behind Which No One Opened (Four Questions for Awareness)
Once upon a time there was a boy who loved to ask questions. He asked his mother, "Why is the grass green?" He asked his father, "Why is the sky blue?" He asked his grandfather, "Why don't the stars fall?"
But one day he asked questions that no one could answer.
He came to his mother and asked:
- Mom, who created people?
Mom smiled and said:
- That's a tough question, son. You better go play.
He came to his dad and asked:
- Dad, why did they create people?
Dad frowned and said:
— Get to work, think less about stupid things.
He came to his grandfather and asked:
- Grandpa, why do we live on Earth and not on another planet?
Grandfather stroked his head and said:
- It just happened that way, grandson.
He came to the teacher and asked:
— Are there any instructions on how to live correctly?
The teacher looked at him sternly and said:
"Open the textbook, it's all written there."
The boy opened his textbook. It covered math, geography, and history. But not a word about how to live.
He went outside, sat down on a bench, and began to think. Other children were running around, playing, laughing. And the boy suddenly realized: none of them knew the answers to these questions either. They simply hadn't thought about them.
And then he decided: "I'll grow up and definitely find the answers. Or at least I'll search for them my whole life. Because not knowing is normal. But not searching is sad."
Question:
Why do you think the adults couldn't answer the boy's questions? Have you ever asked such questions yourself?
MAIN:
Not knowing the answers is normal. Not looking for them is a crime against yourself.
________________


Chapter 7.7 The Story of Uncle Vasya and the State
(The state as one of the aquariums)
Uncle Vasya spent his entire life criticizing the government. The taxes were high, the pensions were small, the roads were bad, and the officials stole.
His neighbor, Uncle Petya, listened and listened and one day asked:
"Vasya, did you marry the state? Did you choose it?"
“No,” Vasya was surprised.
— Did you sign a contract with him?
- No.
"Then why do you expect anything from it? The state is just a system. Like the weather. You don't scold the weather for being rainy, do you? You just take an umbrella."
Uncle Vasya thought about it. It was true. He'd always expected something from the state, but the state hadn't promised him anything.
“What should I do?” he asked.
"Choose your strategy," Petya replied. "You can pay taxes and live by the laws. You can optimize and pay less. You can go into hiding and stay out of the spotlight. Or you can move to another country altogether. But whining is the most useless thing."
Question:
What's better: criticizing the government or choosing a strategy for yourself?
MAIN:
The state is not your father or mother. It's a system. Choose a strategy and live.
________________


Chapter 7.8 The Story of Digital Traces (Digital Traces)
Once upon a time there was a guy named Kolya. He loved to joke around online. He'd post a funny meme, a caustic comment, or a photo from a drinking session.
I thought it was all nonsense and no one would see.
Ten years passed. Kolya had grown up, gotten smarter, and found a good job. Then he was called into his boss's office.
"Kolya," the boss says, "we've decided to promote you. But first, we Googled your name. And do you know what we found?"
Kolya turned pale.
"Those photos from the drinking party, those comments, those jokes. Our partners saw it. They're against your promotion."
Kolya tried to explain that it had been a long time ago, that he had changed, that it was just a joke. But his companions didn't care.
Kolya quit his job, changed his name, and started anew. But old photos still surfaced occasionally.
Question:
Why couldn't Kolya become a boss?
MAIN:
The internet remembers everything. Even what you've forgotten. Think before you write.
________________


Chapter 7.9 A Tale of Octopuses and Shadows (Global Players)
Enormous octopuses once lived. Their tentacles entangled entire countries, infiltrating governments, banks, and factories.
No one saw them, but everyone felt their presence.
In one country, people learned about octopuses and became scared:
— They rule the world! They control everything! We can't do anything!
In another country, people didn't know about octopuses and lived peacefully, but the octopuses still controlled them.
In a third country, people studied octopuses, understood how they moved, and learned to live in such a way as to avoid falling under their tentacles.
"Why aren't you afraid?" the residents of the first country asked them.
"What's there to be afraid of?" they replied. "Octopuses have always been, are, and always will be. We can't defeat them. But we can study their habits and avoid dangerous situations."
Question:
How can we best relate to what we cannot control?
MAIN:
There are global players. Study them, but don't panic. Panic doesn't help.
________________


Chapter 7.10 The Story of the Fisherman and the Stormy River
(Survival strategy in the macro world)
A fisherman was fishing in a turbulent river. All around were whirlpools, rocks, and rapids. The other fishermen were afraid and sat on the bank.
And this fisherman went out in a boat every day and returned with a catch.
"How do you do it?" they asked him.
"I've studied the river," the fisherman replied. "I know where the whirlpools are and where the calm current is. I don't fight the river, I just swim to where it's safe."
— What if the whirlpool gets you?
— I have a life jacket. And I always know where the shore is.
— What if the river changes?
"Then I'll study it again. The river changes, but the laws of flow remain the same."
The fisherman didn't try to remake the river. He simply took its peculiarities into account and followed the right path.
Question:
What does a fisherman do to survive on a raging river?
MAIN:
Don't fight the river. Study it and swim where you need to go.
________________



SECTION 7 SUMMARY
Self-assessment checklist.
· Do I understand the difference between the microworld (inside) and the macroworld (outside)?
· I know the Matryoshka principle: am I inside a family, inside a city, inside a country?
· I understand that each aquarium has its own rules?
· Do I know that 90% of my decisions are actually predetermined?
· I asked myself four main questions about the meaning of life and instructions?
· I understand that the state is simply a system that can be worked with?
· I remember that I have a digital identity online and I need to keep track of it?
· I know that there are global players - large aquariums above states?
· Have I learned the five principles that lead to freedom?
MAIN:
The world is complex, but knowable. Don't be afraid, don't deny it, study it. And build your life within. 
________________


________________


SECTION 8. THE CONCEPT OF A FREE MAN
(Stories about who you really are)
________________
Introduction to the section
The Story of a Doll and a Puppeteer
There lived a doll in a puppet theater. Beautiful, dressed up, with strings.
Every evening, the puppeteer pulled the strings—the doll danced, bowed, cried, and laughed. The audience applauded.
One day the doll asked:
- Who am I really?
"You are my puppet," the puppeteer replied. "I control you."
— What if I don’t want to be controlled?
- Who's asking you?
At night, when the puppeteer fell asleep, the doll cut the strings and ran away.
This morning, for the first time in her life, she went wherever she wanted. She did what she wanted. She said what she wanted.
It was scary. But it was also free.
MAIN:
You have strings too. Try cutting them.
________________


Chapter 8.1 The story of Vova, who forgot who he was
(You are a resident of planet Earth)
Vova was born in one country. Then that country fell apart, and he became a citizen of another. Then he moved to a third country, became a citizen of a third. Then he received citizenship of a fourth.
His passports listed him differently. One said he was Russian, another said he was Jewish, and a third said he was "stateless" for a time.
Vova got confused and went to the sage.
— Tell me, sage, who am I? Russian? German? Jew?
The sage smiled and pointed to the globe.
— Do you see this dot? This is the place where you were born. And this is the place where you live now. And this is where you will be buried. But all of this is on one planet. You are an inhabitant of planet Earth. Everything else is just labels that people have come up with to divide each other.
Vova looked at the globe and suddenly realized: it's true. Borders were drawn by people. But there's only one planet.
Question:
Who are you - a citizen of a country or an inhabitant of the planet?
MAIN:
You are an inhabitant of planet Earth. Everything else is just papers and labels.
________________


Chapter 8.2 The Story of How the Borders Were Drawn
(Where did states come from)
Long ago, people lived without borders. They walked where they wanted, settled where they liked, and traded with whomever they wanted.
Then strong people came and said:
"This land is mine. And this one is mine too. And you will live on it and pay me."
People were surprised:
— Why yours? We've always lived here.
“Because I have a bigger sword,” answered the strong people.
This is how the first states emerged.
Then the powerful people began to agree among themselves:
— Let's draw a line. This river is yours, this forest is mine.
And they did. People who lived on one side of the river became one, and those on the other – another. Even though yesterday they were neighbors.
Centuries have passed. People have forgotten that borders were drawn by strong men with swords. They think borders are natural, that they've always been that way.
But this is not so.
Question:
Who invented the borders between countries?
MAIN:
People drew the boundaries. People can erase them.
________________


Chapter 8.3 The Story of the Bird in the Cage
(Freedom of choice as an innate right)
Once upon a time there lived a bird in a cage. The cage was beautiful, golden, with perches and feeders. The bird ate, drank, and sang every day.
One day the cage door opened.
The bird looked outside. It was scary: wind, rain, predators, cold. But there was also sun, and forest, and other birds.
"To fly or not to fly?" the bird thought.
She could have stayed. The cage was safe, well-fed, and warm. But it was a cage.
She could have flown away. It's scary, hungry, and cold in the wild. But that's freedom.
The bird thought for a long time. Then it finally flew out.
It was hard at first. She froze, starved, and hid from predators. But gradually she learned to survive. And most importantly, she learned to fly. Really, high, far.
And she understood one thing: freedom is not a gift. It is a choice. And this choice must be paid for.
Question:
What did the bird choose—safety or freedom? What would you choose?
MAIN:
Freedom is not a gift. It is a choice. And this choice has a price.
________________


Chapter 8.4. The Story of Four Books on a Shelf
(On the types of law)
In a large library, there were four books on a single shelf. Each one was old, wise, and very important.
The first bookIt was called "Natural Law." It was the oldest. It wasn't written by man—it was written by life itself. It said: "Everyone born on this planet already has the right to breathe, the right to live, the right to go wherever they want, and the right to be themselves. These rights were not given to you. You have them by the very fact of your birth."
This book was written in a language that every living being understands.
The second bookIt was called "State Law." It was big, heavy, covered with seals and signatures. It was written by people who got together and said, "We have agreed to live by these rules." It told how much to pay in taxes, where you could build a house, how to drive on the roads.
This book was important. But it could be rewritten. And people have rewritten it many times.
The third bookIt was called "International Law." It was written in many languages ​​at once. In it, countries agreed with each other not to fight without cause, to help those in trouble, and to respect borders.
This book was like a bridge between different houses. A bridge can be rebuilt, but as long as it exists, it's comfortable to walk across.
The fourth bookIt was called "Corporate Law." It was the smallest and newest. It covered the rules within schools, clubs, and companies. "In our class, we do this," "In our company, we do that."
These rules were created by the people who joined these communities. If you didn't like the rules, you could leave for another place or community.

One dayA boy came to the library. He took the first book from the shelf, "Natural Law," and asked the librarian:
— Is it true? That I have rights simply because I was born?
"It's true," said the librarian. "All lawyers in all countries know this. It's studied in universities."
"Then why don't they tell us about this at school?" the boy asked in surprise.
"Because they teach other subjects at school," the librarian replied. "And you have to find this knowledge yourself. Just like now."
The boy looked at the rest of the books.
— What if I take only the first book and say that I don’t need the rest?
"Then you might get into trouble," the librarian said. "Because the second book is written for everyone who lives on this earth. Even if you haven't read it, it still works. Like the law of gravity: if you don't know about it, you'll still fall if you trip."
“So what should I do?” the boy asked.
"Read all four," the librarian smiled. "Understand where everything comes from. And choose. You can only be a citizen of your country. Or you can remember that first you are a human being, an inhabitant of planet Earth. And only then—a citizen."
"Is this legal?" the boy asked.
"It's not only legal," the librarian replied. "It's written into the constitutions of many countries. They speak separately of 'human beings' and 'citizens.' Because whoever wrote those constitutions knew about the first book."
The boy took all four books and went home.
He didn't mean to rebel. He didn't mean to break the rules. He just wanted to know the whole picture. Because when you know everything, not just what you've been told, you're calmer. You're more confident. And it's harder to control you against your will.
Question:
Did you know there are four types of law? What do legal textbooks say about them? That natural law isn't a fiction, but a concept recognized by all countries?
MAIN:
There are books you get at school. And there are books you have to find yourself. Read them all. And only then make a choice.
________________


Chapter 8.5 The Story of the Four Doors
(Four questions and answers)
One man was searching for the meaning of life. He came to a sage and asked:
— Tell me, why do I live?
The sage led him to a room with four doors.
— Behind every door there is one answer. Choose.
The man opened the first door. A priest sat there and said, "God created you. Live by His laws, and you will find meaning."
The man closed the door. He opened a second one. A scientist stood there: "Evolution created you. There's no point, just live and reproduce."
The Third Door. Philosopher: "There is no meaning, but you can invent it yourself. That is your freedom."
The fourth door. It was empty and had a sign on it: "I don't know. Keep looking."
The man returned to the sage:
— I still haven't found an answer. Which one of them is right?
"Everyone," the sage replied. "And no one. Choose for yourself what to believe. But remember: if you don't choose, others will choose for you."
Question:
Which one of them is right?
What do you believe in?
MAIN:
No one will give you a ready-made meaning. Find it yourself. Or live with someone else's.
________________


Chapter 8.6 The Story of the Book You Were Not Given at Birth (Nobody Gave You an Instruction Book)
A man was born. He grew up. He began to live.
Everything seemed to be fine, but one question tormented him: “How to live correctly?”
He went to his parents—they said one thing. He went to his teachers—they said another. His friends advised a third. The TV broadcast a fourth. The internet screamed a fifth.
The man got confused and went to the elder.
- Father, tell me where I can find a real instruction manual for life?
The old man smiled and handed him a clean notebook.
- Here.
"But there's nothing written here!" the man was surprised.
"Did you think they'd give you a ready-made one?" the elder replied. "Nobody gave you instructions. Your parents gave theirs, your teachers gave theirs, the television gave itss. And you just went ahead and believed them. You should have written them yourself."
The man thought for a moment. He took a notebook and a pen and began to write.
A year later he came to the elder with a notebook full of writing.
- Here are my instructions. I wrote them myself.
"Well done," said the old man. "Now you're truly ready to live."
Question:
Whose instructions are you living by now?
MAIN:
Your life is your instruction manual. Don't let others write it for you.
________________


Chapter 8.7 The Story of a Climber Who Was Afraid of Loneliness (Loneliness as a Basic Reality)
The climber was preparing for his ascent of Everest. He assembled a team, gear, and planned a route.
But just before the start, all the team members refused - some got sick, some got scared, some changed their minds.
The climber was left alone.
"How can I go alone?" he thought. "It's impossible! I need a team, I need support, I need people to back me up."
He almost gave up on the climb. But then he remembered he'd been dreaming about it for 10 years.
And he went. Alone.
It was scary. It was cold. It was hard. But he walked.
He stood alone at the summit, surrounded by nothing but snow, wind, and sky.
And suddenly he realized: he wasn't alone. He'd always been with himself. And other people—they were only there when he let them in. But the most important person in his life was himself.
Having descended, he no longer feared loneliness. He knew: loneliness is not emptiness. It is freedom.
Question:
What was the climber afraid of and what did he realize at the summit?
MAIN:
You come alone and you leave alone. Everything in between is a gift.
Appreciate, but don't cling.
________________


Chapter 8.8 The Story of the Borderless World Map (No Borders)
The geography teacher gave the students an assignment: to draw a map of the world.
One student drew, as usual: countries, borders, capitals.
The second student drew it differently: he erased all the boundaries, leaving only the continents, oceans, mountains, and rivers.
The teacher was surprised:
— Where are the boundaries?
"There aren't any," the student replied. "Borders are invented by people. In reality, they don't exist. There's only land, water, and air."
"But that can't happen!" the classmates protested. "There's Russia, there's America, there's China!"
“Yes,” the student agreed. “Because we agreed that they exist. But if tomorrow we agree differently, the borders will change. They are not eternal. But the earth is eternal.”
The teacher paused. But the boy was right. Boundaries are simply an agreement.
Question:
What is real and what is made up by people?
MAIN:
Borders are only in our heads. In reality, there is only one planet.
________________


Chapter 8.9: The Story of Rules That No One Wrote
(No rules)
In one family there were strict rules: you have to study, you have to work, you have to get married, you have to have children, you have to save for old age.
The son grew up and began to live by these rules. He studied, got a job, got married, had a child, and saved.
But there was no happiness.
He went to see a psychologist:
"Doctor, I'm doing everything right, but I still don't want to live. Why?"
"Who said this is right?" asked the psychologist.
- Well... parents. And in general, everyone lives like that.
— Have you tried living not “like everyone else,” but how you want?
The son paused. He'd never really asked himself what he wanted. He'd always followed other people's rules.
He quit his job, got divorced, went into the forest, and became a hermit. A year later, he returned and opened a workshop making wooden toys.
Happiness has arrived. Money has become less, but happiness has increased.
Question:
What rules do you live by? Are they yours or someone else's?
MAIN:
Rules only exist as long as you believe in them.
Stop believing and they will disappear.
________________




Chapter 8.10 The Story of the Ant Who Searched for Meaning
(There is no meaning in life)
The ant was dragging a straw. It was hard, but he dragged it.
A beetle crawled past and asked:
- Hey, ant, why are you doing this?
- What do you mean, why? I'm building a house.
- Why do you need a house?
— To live.
- Why live?
The ant thought about it. He had never asked himself this question.
“Well... to build a house,” he answered uncertainly.
"Why build a house if you live to build a house?" the beetle chuckled and crawled away.
The ant was left at a loss. The straw fell from its paws. It sat down and thought.
A caterpillar crawled past. It saw the pensive ant and asked:
- What's wrong?
“I’m looking for the meaning of life,” the ant sighed.
"Ah," the caterpillar waved. "I was looking too. And then I realized: there's no meaning. There's just life. And everyone decides for themselves how to fill it."
The ant thought about it and decided: I'll just live. Haul straws, build a house, enjoy the sun. And the point... well, forget it.
Question:
Why do you live?
MAIN:
There is no meaning to life. But you can invent one. And that is your superpower.
________________


Chapter 8.11 The Story of the Doll and the Puppeteer
(You don't belong to anyone)
There was a doll in the puppet theater. A beautiful, elegant one, with strings.
The puppeteer pulled the strings, and the doll danced. He pulled others, and the doll bowed. He pulled others, and the doll cried.
One day the doll got tired of it. She asked:
- Why do I always dance when you pull?
"Because you're my puppet," the puppeteer replied. "I made you, I control you."
- What if I don’t want to?
- Who's asking you?
The doll thought for a moment. But the truth is, no one asks. She simply follows orders.
At night, when the puppeteer fell asleep, the doll cut the strings and ran away.
The puppeteer woke up in the morning, and the doll was gone. He searched and searched, but couldn't find it.
And the doll wandered the world. At first, it was scary—no one was bothering her, and she had no idea what to do. Then she got used to it. And she realized: it's better to be free and responsible for yourself than to be someone else's and dance to someone else's tune.
Question:
Who's pulling your strings?
MAIN:
You are not someone else's property. You belong only to yourself.
Cut the strings.
________________


Chapter 8.12 The Story of the Boy Who Vinyld the Mirror
(Freedom is responsibility)
The boy broke a window with a ball. He ran to his mother and said:
- Mom, this window broke itself!
"How did it happen?" Mom asked in surprise.
- Well, I just threw the ball, and it went and broke.
Mom sighed and led him to the mirror.
— Look. It's a mirror. Tell it: "It's your fault that I look into it."
The boy said.
— Did it answer?
- No.
- Now say: “I decided to look into it myself, because I wanted to see my face.”
The boy said. And suddenly he realized: the mirror had nothing to do with it. He had decided for himself.
"It's the same with the window," Mom said. "You decided to play ball near the house yourself. You threw the ball yourself. The window had nothing to do with it. It's your fault. Or rather, not your fault, but you bear the consequences."
The boy understood. He went to his neighbor, apologized, and offered to help him install the new window.
Question:
Who is to blame for the broken window?
MAIN:
Freedom isn't a gift. It's a deal: you get choice, but you pay with responsibility.
________________


Chapter 8.13 The Story of the Wolf and the Pack
(Live alone or join together)
The wolf lived in a pack. They hunted together, shared their prey together, and defended themselves against enemies together.
But the wolf felt cramped. He longed for independence. He left the pack and began living alone.
At first, everything was fine. No one was telling him what to do, no one was taking his prey. But then winter came. Hunting alone became difficult. The wolf was starving, freezing, and growing weak.
One day, he was attacked by strange wolves. He fought them off, but they were outnumbered. Then his former pack came running—they heard the howling and came to his aid.
The wolf was rescued. He returned to the pack. Freedom was reduced, but survival became easier.
"What do you choose?" asked the leader.
"I'll be both on my own and with you," the wolf replied. "Sometimes I'll leave, sometimes I'll come back. The main thing is to have a choice."
Question:
Is it better to be alone or with others?
MAIN:
You don't have to be with others. But you don't have to be alone. Choose wisely.
________________



SECTION 8 SUMMARY
Self-assessment checklist.
· Do I remember that my only citizenship is planet Earth?
· I know that the borders between countries were drawn by people?
· I understand that freedom of choice is my innate right, not a gift?
· I asked myself four questions: about the meaning, about the creator, about the purpose, about the instructions?
· I realized that no one gave me a manual for life - I write it myself?
· Do I accept loneliness as a basic reality and not as a curse?
· Do I understand that boundaries exist only in my head?
· Do I only follow the rules that I have accepted?
· I know there is no ready-made meaning in life, but can I create it?
· I remember that I do not belong to anyone - neither to the state, nor to my family, nor to anyone else?
· Am I willing to pay for freedom with my responsibility?
· Am I able to choose consciously when to be alone and when to unite with others?
THE MAIN THING (quote from the great Schopenhauer):
"From the moment you're born, your name, country, faith, and identity are already chosen for you. And then you spend your whole life defending what you never chose." Stop defending. Start choosing. 
________________


________________


SECTION 9. FIVE LIFE SCENARIOS
(Stories about where and how to live)
________________
Introduction to the section
The Story of a Map and a Compass
A young man inherited an old map from his grandfather. It marked five places: a forest lake, a mountain village, a port town, a desert oasis, and an island in the middle of the sea. His grandfather had left a note: "In each of these places, you will find happiness in its own way. But the map won't tell you where to go. For that, you need a compass. The compass is inside you."
The guy grabbed his backpack and set off. First, he turned into the woods and lived there for a year in a house by the lake. Then he felt drawn to people and moved to a port city. Two years later, he sold his apartment, bought a camper, and headed for the mountains. He enjoyed changing places. He wasn't choosing "forever." He was just trying things out.
Ten years later, he returned to his grandfather's map. All five spots were marked with checkmarks. He hadn't found the "perfect" one. He realized something else: there is no perfect one, only your choice today. And tomorrow, you can choose again.
MAIN:
Don't look for the one right path. Look for the one that suits you now. Tomorrow the path may change. That's not scary. That's freedom.
________________


Chapter 9.1 The Story of the Hamster in the Wheel
(Diagnosis: Who and why forces us to buy unnecessary things?)
The hamster was running on its wheel. He ran quickly, his paws flashing, his eyes shining.
A wise owl passed by and asked:
- Hamster, where are you running?
"What do you mean, where to?" the hamster was surprised. "I'm running!"
- Why?
— To make the wheel turn!
- Why does the wheel spin?
- So that I run!
The owl shook its head and flew away. And the hamster continued to run in the wheel until old age.
Question:
Who do you look like - a hamster or an owl?
MAIN:
Stop. Look at the wheel. Maybe it's time to get out?
________________


Chapter 9.2 The Tale of Five Roads (Five Scenarios Overview)
A man stood at a crossroads. Five roads ahead of him.
The first one led into the forest. There was a house, a vegetable garden, silence.
The second one led into the city. There were apartments, shops, and bustle.
The third one led into the mountains. There was a camper, freedom, and wind.
The fourth one led to the lake. There was land there and a camper next to it.
The fifth one led to the airport. There were tickets to different countries.
The man stood and thought: which one to choose?
The sage came up and said:
"You can choose one. Or you can go through them all one by one. You can go to the city today, to the mountains tomorrow, to the forest the day after. This is your life. You don't have to choose forever."
The man smiled and went first into the city.
Question:
Which road would you choose?
MAIN:
Five roads. You can walk them all.
________________


Chapter 9.3 The story of a grandfather who went to the village
(Script by A. Usadba)
Grandfather lived in the city his whole life. Noise, dirt, neighbors on the other side of the wall, the constant rush.
He retired and said:
— That's enough. I'm leaving for the village.
The grandchildren twirled their fingers at their temples:
"Grandpa, are you crazy? There's nothing there! No shops, no entertainment, no people!"
"There's land there," the grandfather replied. "And that's the main thing."
He left. Bought a house in an abandoned village. He got chickens, planted a vegetable garden, fixed the stove, and dug a well.
A year later, his grandchildren came to visit him. They saw their grandfather beaming, rosy-cheeked, and cheerful.
- Grandpa, how are you?
"Better than I've ever been in my entire life. Fresh air, my own food, silence. I'm my own boss. No one tells me what to do, I'm not dependent on anyone."
The grandchildren became thoughtful. Maybe their grandfather really wasn't crazy, but the wisest of them all?
Question:
Why did grandfather go to the village and become happier?
MAIN:
The earth feeds. The earth protects. The earth gives freedom.
________________


Chapter 9.4 The Story of Masha, Who Threw Away Half Her Things (Scenario B. City Apartment)
Masha lived in a three-room apartment. The entire apartment was crammed with things. The closets were bursting with clothes, the shelves with books, the drawers with knick-knacks.
Masha spent every Saturday cleaning. Dusting, washing, putting away.
One day a friend came to her and said:
"Masha, you can't live here! What are you, a collector?"
“It’s just that so much has accumulated,” Masha sighed.
— Do you wear all these clothes?
- Well... a hundredth part.
— Do you reread books?
- No.
- Why do you need trinkets?
- Beautiful.
A friend took garbage bags and threw out half of them in an hour.unnecessarythings. Masha screamed at first, but then suddenly realized: it became easier to breathe. And cleaning became faster. And there was more free space.
A month later, Masha threw out half more. And more. Only the essentials remained. And life became simpler.
Question:
How many things do you really need?
MAIN:
The less things, the more life. Free yourself.
________________


Chapter 9.5 The story of Kolya, who went to the camper
(Scenario B. Digital nomadism. Basic level)
Kolya worked in the office. Every day—road, work, road, home, sleep. And so on in a circle.
One day he saw a video on the internet about a guy who lives in a camper van and travels.
"Why can't I do that?" thought Kolya.
I counted the money, sold the apartment, bought a camper van and left.
It was scary at first. I didn't know where to sleep, where to wash, where to get internet. But then I got used to it.
A year later, he'd traveled half the country. He worked from a camper van with a laptop, headphones, and internet access. He freelanced, and the money was enough.
He returned to his hometown and stopped by to visit his friends. They saw Kolya, tanned and calm, his eyes sparkling.
"How are you?" they ask.
"Better than anyone," he replies. "I realized that home isn't a place. Home is a state of being."
Question:
Where was Kolya's house?
MAIN:
Home is not a place. Home is a state of being.
If you are free inside, you are free everywhere.
________________


Chapter 9.5.1 The Story of Andrey and His Three Houses
(Scenario B. Digital Nomadism. Advanced Level)
Alexey spent ten years building an IT company. Once the business got off the ground, he sold his stake.
Friends thought: “Now Alexey will buy a big house and live peacefully.”
But Alexey acted differently.
He bought a small apartment in Portugal, a small house in Thailand for a year, and left the apartment in his hometown.
"Why do you need three houses?" the friends were surprised. "You're just one person."
"If problems start in one country—a crisis, taxes—I'll just move to another," Alexey replied. "My money is in different banks. My assets are in different countries."
"But is it really possible to be everywhere?" the friends asked.
"You can't be everywhere," Alexey smiled. "But you don't have to be dependent on just one place."
Question:
Why didn't Alexey buy one big house, as his friends advised?
MAIN:
Freedom with capital is not about “living wherever you want.”
It's "not losing everything if something goes wrong."
________________


Chapter 9.6 The Story of Peter and His Campervan Plot
(Script by G. Hybrid)
Peter wanted both his own property and freedom of movement. His friends laughed:
- Choose, you can’t have both!
Pyotr didn't listen. He bought a plot of land in a picturesque location. But instead of building a house, he put a camper on the property.
"Are you crazy?" the friends asked in surprise. "This isn't a house, it's a car!"
"It's a mobile home," Peter replied. "I can stay here if I want, or I can leave if I want."
He planted a garden, created a vegetable patch, and installed a greenhouse. And he lives in a camper van.
In the fall, when the harvest is in, Pyotr gets into his camper and heads south. He returns in the spring.
And so it is every year.
"So how is it?" the friends ask.
— Excellent. Both our land and our freedom. No need to choose.
Question:
What is better - to have your own home or to be free?
MAIN:
Don't choose between home and freedom. Take both.
________________


Chapter 9.7 The Story of the Man with Six Flags
(Scenario D. Six Flags)
A very rich man once said:
"I can't live in one country. Taxes are high there, politics is unstable, the climate is bad."
He bought a house in Switzerland for money. An apartment in London for business. A villa in Italy for the summer. A small house in Thailand for the winter. A mansion in the US for the children's education. And he left behind an apartment in Moscow so he would have somewhere to return to.
He had six flags. Six countries where he could live.
When there was a crisis in one country, he simply moved to another. When there were tax problems in one, he transferred money to another. When it was winter in one, he flew to where it was summer.
Friends asked:
- Where is your home?
“Where I am now,” he answered. “And home is the entire planet.”
Question:
Where is this man's home?
MAIN:
One country, one point of failure. Six countries, almost invulnerability.
________________


Chapter 9.8 A Story of Choice
(Comparison of scenarios: how to choose)
One day, five people got together and started arguing about who was living better.
The first one says:
— I live on an estate. I have my own land, my own food, clean air. Independence!
The second one says:
— And I'm in the city. Work is nearby, shops are nearby, people are nearby. Convenient!
The third one says:
— And I'm in a campervan. Here today, there tomorrow. Freedom!
The fourth says:
— And I have land, and a camper van. And my own property, and freedom.
The fifth one says:
— And I live in six countries. I live wherever I want.
They argued for a long time, but still couldn't reach an agreement. Then a sage came and said:
— Each of you is right. For yourself. There is no perfect scenario. There is one that suits you.
Question:
Which scenario suits you?
MAIN:
There is no right choice. There is your choice. Make it and don't look back.
________________


Chapter 9.9 A Story of the Common (What all scenarios have in common)
Five people lived differently.
One is on a farm, another is in the city, the third is in a motorhome, the fourth is in a hybrid, and the fifth is in six countries.
One day they met and began to look for something in common.
“I don’t have many things,” said the first one.
“Me too,” said the second.
“Me too,” said the others.
“I have an airbag,” said the second.
“And I have it too,” everyone said.
“I’m not tied to one place of work,” said the third.
“And me,” said the others.
“I chose this way of life myself,” said the fourth.
“And me too,” everyone said.
“I’m ready to change the script if necessary,” said the fifth.
“And me,” said the others.
"But we have everything in common!" they were surprised. "We just live differently, but the meanings are the same."
Question:
What do all five scenarios have in common?
MAIN:
The scenarios vary, but the essence is the same: freedom. Seek the essence, not the form.
________________


Chapter 9.10 A Story of Combinations (Scenario Combinations)
One woman couldn't decide on a life plan. She wanted to live on a farm, then in the city, then in a camper.
She went to the sage and asked:
— How should I choose? I can’t decide!
"Why choose?" the sage asked in surprise.
- Why? We need just one thing!
"Who said that?" the sage smiled. "Live in the city, save up for a homestead. Then buy some land, put your RV there, and test it out. If it works, build a house. If not, travel the world in your RV and look for a place for your next base."
The woman did just that. And her life began to feel like a fascinating journey, not a painful choice.
Question:
Do you have to choose just one?
MAIN:
You don't have to make a choice once and for all. You can try and change.
________________
Chapter 9.11 The Story of the Scary Aunt
(The main fear and how to deal with it)
One man was very afraid of changing his life. He kept seeing a scary woman who said:
— What if it doesn't work out? What if you regret it? What if you lose everything?
He sat in place and changed nothing. And life passed him by.
One day he met a friend who had started living in a campervan and traveling ten years ago.
“How are you?” he asked.
“Excellent,” replied the friend.
— Were you scared?
- Was.
- What did you do?
— I took out a financial safety net. Just in case. "Plan B."
— And if it hadn’t worked out?
— I would have come back. I left money for the return trip.
The man paused to think. It turns out fear is just a tiny creature. It can be driven away with a flashlight called "Plan B."
Question:
What do you fear most when you think about changing your life?
MAIN:
It's better to try and regret than not to try and regret all your life.
________________



SECTION 9 SUMMARY
Self-assessment checklist.
· I know FIVE LIFE SCENARIOS?
Scenario A (Estate) – my own home on the land, autonomy. Is it right for me?
Scenario B (City) – minimalism in the apartment. Is it right for me?
Scenario B (Nomadism) – life on the move, RV. Is it right for me?
Scenario G (Hybrid) – land plus a motorhome. Is it right for me?
Scenario D (Six Flags) – multiple bases in different countries. Is it right for me?
· Have I chosen a scenario to start?
· Do I understand what all the scenarios have in common?
· Do I know how to combine scenarios?
· Have I worked through my biggest fear?
MAIN:
Choose your own scenario. Or someone else will choose for you. 
________________
